Yallourn Open Cut is one of the three vast brown coal mines of Victoria’s Latrobe Valley, alongside Hazelwood and Loy Yang, forming a mining complex that covers more than 50 square kilometres—an area comparable to Sydney Harbour. The sheer scale of these open-cut mines is rarely appreciated beyond fleeting roadside or rail-line views, meaning their long-term planning, environmental management, and rehabilitation often remain out of sight, out of mind for the wider public. Geologically, the Latrobe Valley hosts around a quarter of the world’s known brown coal reserves, estimated at about 65 billion tonnes. The coal occurs in five major seams within the Traralgon, Morwell, and Yallourn formations, with individual seams reaching thicknesses of up to 100 metres. These coal measures are overlain by younger riverine sediments of the Boisdale and Haunted Hills formations, deposited around three million years ago during periods of falling sea levels, creating a complex veneer of gravels, sands, and silts that locally erode the coal. Brown coal mining began in the region in the late nineteenth century, with Yallourn operating continuously since 1921 and currently supplying fuel to electricity generation. Despite more than a century of mining, none of the major open-cut areas has been fully rehabilitated and returned to non-mining use. At Yallourn, rehabilitation works have improved stability but remain closed to public access, while at Hazelwood and Loy Yang internal dumping of overburden was delayed for decades, raising concerns about long-term landform stability. Ongoing controversies focus on the absence of proven end uses, the likelihood that mine voids will remain as partially filled socalled sunken landscapes and fears that without ambitious rehabilitation aimed at environmental, community, and economic value, these enormous sites may become enduring environmental liabilities rather than assets.

The Brumagen Open Cut, located near Stawell in Victoria’s Northern Grampians, is a prominent legacy mining site closely associated with the historic Stawell Gold Mine. It forms part of a landscape shaped by successive waves of pastoral settlement, gold rushes, and industrial mining that transformed the district from grazing land into one of Victoria’s most important inland goldfields. Brumagen represents the later, large-scale open-cut phase of mining that followed earlier underground and shaft-based extraction during the Victorian Gold Rush, and today stands as both an industrial relic and a marker of the region’s economic development. Geologically, the Brumagen Open Cut lies within the Lachlan Fold Belt, a vast and ancient geological province extending across southeastern Australia. Formed during the Palaeozoic Era by intense folding, faulting, and magmatic activity associated with convergent plate margins, the Lachlan Fold Belt hosts complex rock structures and rich mineralisation. It is internationally recognised for economically significant deposits of gold, copper, silver, lead, zinc, and tin, and has produced some of Australia’s most important goldfields. Around Stawell, gold was discovered in quartz reefs in the 1850s, particularly along the so-called “Stawell Golden Mile,” leading to deep underground mining that later evolved into more mechanised, open-cut operations as technology advanced and shallower deposits were exhausted. Today, the Brumagen Open Cut is inactive and forms part of the Stawell Gold Mine, operated by Stawell Gold Mines Pty Ltd and indirectly owned by Arete Capital Partners. Current mining activity is focused underground, and the operator has indicated no intention to redevelop the Brumagen or Big Hill open-cut areas, instead discussing with Earth Resources Regulation the potential removal of these areas from the mining licence for future community use. Ongoing issues and controversies relate to ownership disputes among investors, long-term land use and rehabilitation outcomes, and community expectations about how this historically significant but dormant site should be managed and repurposed.

site_location: Mount Brockman, Ashburton Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Brockman No. 2 Mine is a large open-cut iron ore operation in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 60 kilometres north-west of Tom Price in the Hamersley Range, one of the world’s premier iron ore provinces. Operating since 1992, the mine is fully owned and run by Rio Tinto Iron Ore through its subsidiary Hamersley Iron Pty Ltd and forms part of Rio Tinto’s extensive Pilbara network of twelve mines. Brockman No. 2 has a production capacity of around 8.7 million tonnes of lump and fines iron ore per year. Ore is mined using conventional open-cut, truck-and-shovel methods, crushed and screened on site, and then transported by rail to the port of Dampier for blending and export. The workforce operates on a fly-in fly-out basis, supported by the nearby Brockman Airport, and the mine has experienced periods of closure, including mothballing during the global financial crisis in 2008. Geologically, the mine exploits the Brockman Iron Formation, part of the Hamersley Group, which hosts some of Australia’s most significant iron ore deposits. Mineralisation occurs as bedded iron ore within the Dales Gorge Member and as high-grade detrital ore derived from the weathering of banded iron formations. These rocks, composed mainly of hematite, magnetite, and chert, are folded into large synclinal structures, including the Brockman Syncline, and are interbedded with shale units that influence mining geometry and ore quality. Beyond its technical and economic importance, Brockman No. 2 sits within a broader landscape of social and political controversy in the Pilbara. The mine is located in a region marked by ongoing disputes over Aboriginal heritage protection, land rights, and environmental impacts. These issues, particularly affecting the Yindjibarndi people, highlight systemic weaknesses in mining approvals, cumulative impacts on cultural landscapes, water resources, and the enduring tensions between large-scale resource extraction and the rights of Traditional Owners.

site_location: Mount Brockman, Ashburton Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Nammuldi Mine is a large open-cut iron ore operation in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 60 kilometres north-west of Tom Price in the Hamersley Range. It is wholly owned and operated by Rio Tinto Iron Ore through its subsidiary Hamersley Iron Pty Ltd and forms part of the company’s extensive Pilbara mining network. Production began in 2006 and the mine has an output of approximately 6.6 million tonnes of iron ore per year. Nammuldi is notable for its role in technological change in mining: from 2015, all haul trucks at Nammuldi and the nearby Yandicoogina mine have been operated remotely from Perth, around 1,200 kilometres away, making them the first fully remote-controlled iron ore trucking operations in the world. Geologically, the mine sits within the Hamersley Iron Province, an approximately 80,000-square-kilometre region that contains about 80 per cent of Australia’s identified iron ore reserves. The rocks belong to the Hamersley Group, formed around 2.5 billion years ago through chemical sedimentation in ancient oceans, with later volcanic and sedimentary inputs. Mining at Nammuldi targets the Marra Mamba Iron Formation, composed of banded iron formations rich in hematite and goethite, with alternating silica-rich layers. Nearby deposits also exploit the overlying Brockman Iron Formation, together forming some of the highest-grade iron ore resources globally. Despite its economic and technological significance, Nammuldi has been affected by controversy over Aboriginal cultural heritage protection. In August 2023, blasting near the mine damaged part of an ancient rock shelter estimated to be 40,000–50,000 years old, causing distress among the Muntulgura Guruma people. Disputes arose over the extent of the damage and whether the company adequately acknowledged the impact, reviving broader concerns about mining approvals and cultural heritage safeguards in the Pilbara.

The Iron King Mine was a pyrite mining operation located about six kilometres south of Norseman in the Goldfields–Esperance region of Western Australia, developed to support Australia’s agricultural industry rather than gold production. Its establishment was driven by Commonwealth Government policy to encourage domestic sulphur production for sulphuric acid used in superphosphate fertiliser, implemented through the Sulphuric Bounty Act of 1954 and the Pyrite Bounty Act of 1960. Operated by Central Norseman Gold, which also ran nearby gold mines, Iron King began production in 1945, with ore transported to the Phoenix mill near Norseman after it was converted from gold to pyrite processing. Geologically, the Iron King deposit consisted of massive pyrite ore. Pyrite, chemically expressed as iron disulfide, is composed of iron and sulphur, with sulphur forming a fundamental part of the crystal structure. At Iron King, the ore averaged around 25 per cent sulphur, with estimated reserves of approximately 735,000 tonnes above a 25 per cent sulphur cut-off. Unlike the surrounding gold deposits that made Norseman one of Western Australia’s richest historic goldfields, Iron King was valued for its sulphur content rather than precious metals. The mine is remembered as much for its problems as for its production. It developed a poor safety record, with multiple fatalities recorded between the 1940s and 1950s, including a fatal fall down the main shaft in 1954. Economically, Iron King was never competitive without government support, and when cheaper sulphur became available through the processing of sour natural gas overseas, the subsidy was withdrawn. The mine closed abruptly on 31 May 1972, the same day the bounty ended. More broadly, Norseman has continued to experience mining-related safety challenges, with several fatal accidents at nearby operations in recent decades, reinforcing ongoing debates about worker safety, regulation, and the long-term vulnerabilities of small, mining-dependent communities.

Mount Webber is an open-cut iron ore mine in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 190 kilometres south-south-east of Port Hedland along the Abydos–Marble Bar Road near the boundary of the East Pilbara and Port Hedland shires. The mine was developed as a joint venture between Atlas Iron Limited and Altura Mining Limited, after Atlas acquired the iron ore rights from Haoma Mining in 2012, while Haoma retained rights to non-iron minerals. Construction cost approximately Australian dollars 210–212 million, and mining commenced in mid-2014, making Mount Webber Atlas Iron’s fifth Pilbara operation and, for a time, its primary producing asset. Ore was trucked to Port Hedland for export, with production initially around 3 million tonnes per annum and later targeted to rise to about 6 million tonnes per annum. Geologically, Mount Webber lies within the Chichester sub-region of the Pilbara, characterised by gently undulating granite and basalt plains overlain by alluvial sands, silts, and gravels. The deposit comprises the Ibanez, Fender, and Gibson ore bodies and hosts high-grade hematite mineralisation. Reported Joint Ore Reserves Committee figures indicate reserves of roughly 30 million tonnes grading about 57 per cent iron, with earlier estimates placing proven and probable reserves closer to 34 million tonnes at slightly lower grades. Mining was conducted by conventional open-pit drill, blast, load, and haul methods, with on-site crushing and screening before haulage. Mount Webber’s history has been closely tied to volatility in global iron ore markets. Less than a year after opening, operations were suspended in 2015 due to falling prices linked to oversupply and slower Chinese demand, before restarting following royalty relief from the Western Australian Government and cost reductions. Atlas Iron was later acquired by Hancock Prospecting in 2018. As Atlas’ other Pilbara mines were exhausted, Mount Webber remained one of its last operating sites, but declining production ultimately led to closure in 2024, underscoring ongoing debates about the sustainability of small, price-sensitive iron ore operations.

site_location: Mabel Downs Station, Halls Creek Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Sally Malay Mine, now known as the Savannah Nickel–Copper–Cobalt Mine, is a combined open-cut and underground mining operation located about 104 kilometres north-north-east of Halls Creek in the Kimberley region of Western Australia. Operated by Panoramic Resources, Savannah is unusual in that it is one of the very few nickel mines in the Kimberley, as most of Western Australia’s nickel production occurs in the Goldfields–Esperance region. Since commencing operations in the mid-2000s, the mine has experienced multiple cycles of expansion, closure, and restart, reflecting the volatility of global nickel markets. Most recently, it was placed into care and maintenance in early 2024 after Panoramic Resources entered voluntary administration, with falling nickel prices cited as the principal cause. Geologically, the Savannah deposit hosts nickel, copper, and cobalt sulphide mineralisation, initially mined by open pit and later by underground methods as operations deepened. The ore body supported significant production over time, and during its early years Savannah became the largest producer of copper in Western Australia, at times supplying up to half of the state’s output. Over its first twelve years of operation, the mine produced approximately 94,600 tonnes of nickel, 53,000 tonnes of copper, and 5,000 tonnes of cobalt. Concentrates have been exported through the port of Wyndham, with long-term offtake agreements supplying Asian markets, particularly China. Beyond economic and operational challenges, the mine has been associated with social and cultural controversy. Indigenous representatives objected to the original use of the name “Sally Malay,” arguing it breached Aboriginal cultural protocols concerning the naming of deceased people. An elder’s family publicly opposed the name as disrespectful, prompting the company’s decision to rebrand the operation as the Savannah Mine. This dispute, alongside repeated closures and workforce redundancies, highlights the complex intersection of resource development, market forces, and cultural respect in remote northern Australia.

site_location: Spotted Quoll Deposit, Kondinin Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Tim King pit is the original open-cut mine at the Spotted Quoll nickel operation, part of the Forrestania nickel district in southern Western Australia, about 80 kilometres east-southeast of Hyden. Spotted Quoll, together with the nearby Flying Fox mine, forms the Forrestania Operation, now owned and operated by IGO Limited following its takeover of Western Areas in 2022. The Tim King open pit was mined between 2009 and early 2012 and proved highly successful, delivering strong production at low cash costs. It was completed in February 2012, almost six weeks ahead of schedule, and provided the foundation for a smooth transition to underground mining, which commenced in late 2011 and rapidly ramped up toward a production rate of around 10,000 tonnes of nickel per annum. Geologically, the Spotted Quoll deposit is a high-grade nickel sulphide system typical of the Forrestania greenstone belt. Mineralisation occurs within ultramafic host rocks and includes oxidised, transitional, and fresh sulphide ores. The Tim King open pit produced approximately 488,000 tonnes of ore at an average grade of 5.8 per cent nickel, containing more than 28,500 tonnes of nickel. As mining progressed to depth, underground extraction targeted higher-grade sulphide zones accessed via the Hanna decline. Ore from Spotted Quoll and Flying Fox is processed at the nearby Cosmic Boy Concentrator, a central facility expanded to support multiple mines in the district. Additional issues surrounding Spotted Quoll relate less to controversy than to market and operational pressures. Expansion plans across Forrestania were slowed when global nickel prices declined in the early 2010s, and employment levels fluctuated accordingly. More recently, the operation has drawn attention for its location on Native Title lands of the Maduwongga, Marlinyu Ghoolie, and Ngadju peoples, highlighting the urgent need to balance high-grade nickel production with respectful engagement, land access agreements, and long-term mine closure planning in a mature mining district.

site_location: Bronzewing Goldfield, Leonora Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Challenger Mine is a remote gold mining operation in the Far North of South Australia, located about 165 kilometres west of the Stuart Highway and roughly 740 kilometres north-west of Adelaide, within the Woomera Prohibited Area. Discovered in 1995 by Dominion Mining and named by the discovery geologist after his dog, Challenger developed into South Australia’s most significant hard-rock gold mine. Mining began as an open pit in 2002 and transitioned to underground operations in 2005, supported by extensive on-site infrastructure including a processing plant, airstrip, village, power stations, and water bore field. Over its life, the mine produced more than one million ounces of gold before operations ceased in 2018. Ownership changed several times—from Dominion to Kingsgate and later to WPG Resources—and the mine is currently in care and maintenance, with revival efforts under consideration. Geologically, the Challenger deposit lies within the Gawler Craton, a stable crystalline basement province formed between the late Archaean and Mesoproterozoic eras. Gold mineralisation occurs within Christie gneiss of the Mulgathing Complex, hosted by silica- and feldspar-rich greisenised pegmatite veins that are tightly folded and shear-controlled. The orebody is characterised by lens-shaped, anastomosing shoots plunging at approximately 45 degrees, remaining open at depth with continuity demonstrated to at least 2.8 kilometres down-plunge. High-grade zones commonly contain coarse visible gold associated with sulphide minerals such as arsenopyrite and pyrrhotite, while competent host rocks and dry underground conditions simplified mining. Beyond geology, Challenger illustrates both technical success and financial vulnerability. Despite strong grades and favourable mining conditions, repeated ownership changes, rising costs, and corporate insolvency led to suspension of operations in 2018. Its location within a restricted defence area added logistical complexity, and the site’s long-term future remains uncertain, highlighting the challenges of sustaining large, remote underground mines despite world-class geology.

Cams Open Cut is a gold mine located in the Shire of Leonora in the remote north-eastern Goldfields of Western Australia, a region long defined by mineral extraction. The mine forms part of a broader cluster of operations that includes the Bellevue Gold Mine and the historically significant Sons of Gwalia mine, one of Australia’s largest and deepest gold mines. Mining has shaped Leonora since gold discoveries in 1896 led to the establishment of the twin townships of Leonora and Gwalia. Although the closure of Sons of Gwalia in 1963 caused severe population decline in Gwalia, Leonora survived as an administrative, rail, and supply centre. Renewed gold production from the 1980s onward, along with nickel mining at Leinster, has sustained the region into the present. Geologically, Cams Open Cut lies within the Archaean greenstone belts of the Yilgarn Craton, which host some of Australia’s richest gold and nickel deposits. The local geology includes amphibolite, basalt, ironstone, and quartzite, rock types commonly associated with structurally controlled gold mineralisation. These greenstone sequences, extensively explored since the early twentieth century following surveys by government geologists, continue to attract modern mining investment. Despite its economic importance, mining around Leonora has generated persistent social and political tensions. Aboriginal communities have experienced long-term disruption through land dispossession and damage to cultural heritage sites, with ongoing disputes over participation and decision-making power. Broader controversies include debates over a proposed radioactive waste facility in 2018, concerns about the financial burden placed on small local governments, and community unease over fly-in, fly-out workforces. Together, these issues highlight the enduring conflict between large-scale resource development and the sustainability and character of small regional communities.

site_location: Paroo Station Project, Wiluna, Wiluna Shire, Western Australia
metadata: Rosslyn Hill Mining, formerly known as Magellan Metals, operates the Paroo Station Mine near Wiluna in Western Australia. The mine is an open-cut lead operation producing cerussite (lead carbonate) concentrate. Rosslyn Hill Mining is a subsidiary of LeadFX, a Canadian-based base-metals company formerly listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange. During operations, lead concentrate was transported by road and rail in sealed shipping containers to the Port of Fremantle for export. Since its establishment in 1996, the company has experienced repeated interruptions due to market conditions, regulatory scrutiny, and community concern, and the mine has spent long periods in care and maintenance. Geologically, Paroo Station Mine exploits near-surface secondary lead mineralisation dominated by cerussite, formed through the weathering of primary lead sulphide deposits. The deposit is amenable to open-cut mining and on-site concentration. Its location in the Wiluna region places it within a broader mineral province that also hosts gold and iron ore deposits, enabling shared infrastructure with nearby projects. The operation is best known for its role in the Esperance lead contamination incident of 2006–2007, when fugitive lead dust from bulk handling caused bird deaths, elevated blood-lead levels in residents, and widespread environmental contamination. A parliamentary inquiry followed, and the Government of Western Australia spent about $20 million on cleanup, with Magellan contributing voluntarily to remediation and community projects. In response, the company developed a sealed shipment process, later endorsed by the Environmental Protection Authority as best practice. Despite approvals, further transport incidents and depressed lead prices led to repeated shutdowns. Efforts to restart the mine through a hydrometallurgical plant producing high-purity lead ingots gained regulatory support but failed to secure funding. In December 2024, Rosslyn Hill Mining entered voluntary administration, with port access approvals expiring and no viable export pathway in place.

The Argyle Diamond Mine was a major diamond operation located in the remote East Kimberley region of northern Western Australia. Discovered in 1979 by the Ashton Joint Venture after years of exploration, the mine commenced alluvial production in 1983, open-pit mining in 1985, and underground mining in 2013. Operated by Rio Tinto, Argyle became the world’s largest producer of natural diamonds by volume, yielding more than 865 million carats of rough diamonds before operations ceased in November 2020. Although only about 5 percent of production was gem quality, Argyle supplied between 90 and 95 percent of the world’s highly prized pink diamonds, as well as rare red, purple, and blue stones, making it globally significant despite its low average diamond value. Geologically, Argyle was exceptional as the first successful commercial diamond mine exploiting a lamproite pipe rather than the more common kimberlite. The AK1 pipe is a diatreme composed of olivine lamproite tuffs and lavas, formed by an explosive volcanic eruption beneath shallow water. The highest diamond grades occurred in sandy tuff units, which contain volcanic and sedimentary clasts in a recrystallised matrix. Diamonds are predominantly eclogitic in origin and unusually small, often showing etched surfaces and distorted crystal forms. The diamonds formed around 1.58 billion years ago, while the volcanic pipe itself is younger, dating to about 1.1–1.2 billion years. Beyond geology, Argyle is notable for its secrecy during discovery, innovative marketing of brown diamonds as “champagne” and “cognac,” and its economic model based on very high ore grades rather than stone value. Controversies centred on environmental management, rising underground costs, and eventual economic non-viability. Since closure, Rio Tinto has focused on long-term decommissioning and rehabilitation in partnership with Traditional Owners, the Miriuwung and Gija peoples. Throughout the life of the mine, these Traditional Owners maintained cultural and spiritual connections to the land despite extensive industrial development. Following closure in 2020, Rio Tinto committed to working with the Miriuwung and Gija Traditional Owners on mine rehabilitation.

site_location: Oberdorf magnesite deposit, Oberdorf, Tragöß-Sankt Katharein, Bruck-Mürzzuschlag District, Styria
metadata: The Kaintaleck Mine at Hohenburg, Styria, Austria, is a historic magnesite mining site known for hosting a very pure natural occurrence of magnesite. First documented in detail in the early twentieth century, the deposit at Wiesergut within the Kaintaleck area became a reference locality for the study of anhydrous magnesium carbonate and played an important role in the development of both industrial mining and scientific theories on magnesite formation. Austria has long been central to global magnesite research and production, with discoveries in the former Austro-Hungarian Empire establishing the country as the world’s leading exporter for many decades. The Kaintaleck deposit, together with the nearby Arzberg occurrence, was reinterpreted by Redlich in 1921 as epigenetic rather than sedimentary in origin. He proposed that magnesium-rich fluids altered pre-existing limestones along fractures and fissures, producing massive magnesite with minimal calcium and iron impurities, particularly at Wiesergut. These interpretations fed into the long-standing “magnesite problem,” which questioned whether magnesite could form at low temperatures near Earth’s surface. For more than two centuries, the lack of laboratory synthesis at ambient conditions supported hydrothermal or metamorphic models. Recent experimental breakthroughs, however, have demonstrated reproducible low-temperature magnesite formation, reopening debate over sedimentary and diagenetic pathways and reframing classic Austrian deposits such as Kaintaleck. Beyond geology, the broader Styrian magnesite industry, including operations at Oberdorf an der Laming, has been technologically influential, pioneering advanced furnaces and producing materials for refractories, industrial flooring, and chemicals. Today, magnesite is also attracting attention for its potential role in carbon dioxide sequestration, although translating experimental success into large-scale application remains unresolved.

ArcelorMittal’s Serra Azul Mine is a major iron ore operation located in Itatiaiuçu, in the state of Minas Gerais, Brazil. The mine produces friable itabirite iron ore and forms part of ArcelorMittal Brazil’s strategic supply chain. In recent years, the operation has attracted substantial investment aimed at expanding production capacity and supporting the construction of a pellet plant to produce direct reduction feed, supplying both domestic steelmaking and ArcelorMittal’s pellet facilities abroad, including in Mexico. Alongside expansion, the mine has become a focal point for dam safety reform in Brazil. Geologically, Serra Azul is situated within the Quadrilátero Ferrífero (Iron Quadrangle), one of the world’s most important iron ore provinces. The ore is primarily friable itabirite, a metamorphosed banded iron formation consisting of iron oxides interlayered with silica-rich bands. Friable itabirite is relatively soft and amenable to beneficiation, making it suitable for pellet feed production. The region’s geology has supported more than a century of iron ore mining but is also associated with extensive tailings storage infrastructure. Beyond geology and production, Serra Azul has been marked by significant controversy related to its inactive tailings dam. Although the dam has not received tailings since 2012, concerns escalated after the catastrophic Brumadinho dam failure in 2019. Approximately 200 nearby residents were evacuated when consultants declined to certify the dam’s stability, creating fear, displacement, and prolonged community distress. Critics highlighted regulatory gaps, transparency issues, and disputes over compensation. In response, ArcelorMittal implemented extensive safety upgrades and, in 2025, received environmental approval to begin controlled decommissioning of the dam. The process, governed by agreements with federal and state authorities, is scheduled for completion by 2032 and emphasizes containment structures, environmental remediation, and long-term risk reduction, reflecting a broader shift toward responsible mining and dam closure practices in Brazil.

site_location: Buhovo, Stolichna Municipality, Sofia City Province
metadata: The Buhovo Uranium Mine was one of Bulgaria’s earliest and most important uranium mining centres, located near the town of Buhovo about 22 kilometres northeast of Sofia. Mining began in 1938, initially under German control during the Second World War, and expanded significantly after 1945 when Bulgaria fell within the Soviet sphere of influence. The mine became part of a Soviet-Bulgarian mining enterprise, supplying uranium ore that contributed to the Soviet nuclear program, including the first Soviet atomic bomb. Operations continued through the communist period and ceased in 1992 by government decree, after more than five decades of extraction that transformed the area from an agricultural landscape into a heavily industrialised zone. Geologically, the Buhovo ore field contains several types of uranium-bearing deposits. The largest deposits are structurally controlled by semi-ring fault zones related to the emplacement of the Seslavci–Buhovo intrusion during Hercynian tectonism. Additional stratiform polymetallic, copper, and uranium mineralisation occurs in Mesozoic sedimentary rocks. These younger deposits formed during Eocene tectonic reactivation, when older Palaeozoic uranium accumulations were locally remobilised and redeposited into the sedimentary cover, creating a complex, multi-stage ore system. Beyond its geological and strategic importance, Buhovo is associated with severe environmental and social controversy. Mining relied on poorly protected labour, including prisoners and conscripts, with little awareness of radiation hazards. Widespread contamination of soils, water, and air persists, compounded by nearby steel production at Kremikovci. Agriculture was banned in the area during the 1980s, and radioactive dust continues to affect surrounding communities, making Buhovo a lasting symbol of the environmental and human costs of Cold War uranium mining.

site_location: Kremikovtsi, Stolichna Municipality, Sofia City Province
metadata: The Kremikovtsi Mine is a long-operated open-pit mining complex located about 15 kilometres northeast of Sofia, Bulgaria, on the southern slopes of the Stara Planina Mountains at an elevation of roughly 400 metres. Exploited for more than 50 years, the mine supplied iron ore and barite to the nearby Kremikovtsi metallurgical plant, one of Bulgaria’s largest heavy-industrial projects of the socialist period. Conceived in 1948 as part of the Bulgarian Communist Party’s drive toward rapid industrialisation, the mine and metallurgical complex became symbols of national development. Since 2009, the mine has been privately owned by an Indian steel magnate. Geologically, the deposit is a carbonate-hosted sedimentary exhalative system containing iron, manganese, barite, and sulfides. Mineralisation occurs within carbonate rocks and reflects hydrothermal fluids vented onto or near the seafloor, later preserved and modified through tectonic and sedimentary processes. The deposit’s position along the margins of the Sofia Valley and Stara Planina foothills also meant that mining intersected complex groundwater systems spanning Triassic to Holocene strata. Beyond its geology, Kremikovtsi is marked by deep social and environmental controversy. Mine expansion caused repeated worker fatalities, which local narratives often linked to the demolition of an Orthodox church viewed as sacrilegious. Large-scale excavation displaced residents, triggered housing crises, and reshaped village demographics through state-directed migration. Hydrological disruption redirected ancient groundwater flows into industrial use, while polluted water and dust entered local food and bodies. These combined effects make Kremikovtsi a powerful case study of how extractive geology, industrial ambition, and community life became inseparably entangled under socialist and post-socialist transformation.

site_location: Williams Lake, Cariboo Mining Division, British Columbia
metadata: The Gibraltar Mine is a large copper–molybdenum operation located near McLeese Lake in south-central British Columbia, Canada, about 60 kilometres north of Williams Lake. Owned and operated by Taseko Mines, it is the second largest open-pit copper mine in Canada and the fourth largest in North America, as well as the largest private employer in the Cariboo region. Originally developed by Placer Development Limited in 1972, the mine passed through several ownership changes before being acquired by Taseko in 1999 and fully reopened by 2006. Since then, major expansion phases have increased processing capacity to about 85,000 tonnes per day, supported by substantial capital investment. Geologically, Gibraltar is a porphyry copper–molybdenum deposit, typical of British Columbia’s Interior Plateau. Mineralisation occurs in large, low-grade intrusive systems, where copper and molybdenum are disseminated through altered host rocks. The scale of the deposit makes it suitable for bulk open-pit mining, with reserves reported in the billions of pounds of copper and tens of millions of pounds of molybdenum. Despite its economic importance, the Gibraltar Mine is highly controversial. The mine lies within the traditional territory of the Tŝilhqot’in Nation, including the caretaker area of the ʔEsdilagh First Nation (Alexandria), and was developed without prior consent or a formal environmental assessment. A key issue is the discharge of untreated or minimally treated effluent from the tailings pond into the Fraser River, close to Indigenous communities and traditional fishing sites. The Tŝilhqot’in Nation argues this practice threatens water quality, fish, and cultural survival. In response, ʔEsdilagh First Nation enacted the Sturgeon River Law in 2020, asserting Indigenous legal authority over water protection. Ongoing legal appeals against expanded discharge permits highlight unresolved conflicts over Indigenous rights, environmental protection, and regulatory oversight.

The Chisel Open Pit is a former open-pit mine in the Snow Lake district of northern Manitoba, Canada, where copper and zinc were mined as part of the region’s long history of base-metal production. Mining near Snow Lake began in the mid-twentieth century and was dominated for decades by Hudson Bay Mining and Smelting Company (later Hudbay Minerals). The Chisel Lake open pit, located southwest of the town, produced copper and zinc beginning in 1960, followed by additional mines at Stall Lake, Osborne Lake, and later Chisel North, which became a high-grade zinc producer. By the late 1980s most mines were depleted, and by 1990 Hudbay had ceased operations in the immediate Snow Lake area. Geologically, the Snow Lake district is renowned for volcanogenic massive sulphide (VMS) deposits hosted in Paleoproterozoic volcanic sequences. Two principal types occur: copper–zinc–rich deposits in the Anderson sequence and zinc–copper–lead–silver–rich deposits in the Chisel sequence. These deposits formed from hydrothermal fluids vented along synvolcanic faults, producing massive sulphide lenses dominated by pyrite, pyrrhotite, chalcopyrite, sphalerite, and galena, underlain by stringer stockwork zones. The district also hosts structurally controlled lode-gold vein systems within Amisk Group volcanic rocks, associated with shear zones and lithological contrasts. Despite its economic importance, the Chisel Open Pit is controversial due to its toxic legacy. Abandoned shafts, polychlorinated biphenyl contamination, diesel spills, and elevated arsenic levels in Chisel Lake have raised long-standing concerns. First Nations and local communities have expressed frustration over slow remediation by provincial authorities. Although mine closures were largely driven by economics, environmental cleanup and site stabilization remain unresolved, making Chisel a prominent example of the enduring environmental costs of historic mining and failure of taking responsibility to ameliorate them.

site_location: Moab-Setting Lakes area, Thompson Nickel Belt, Manitoba
metadata: The Thompson Mine is a major, now underground, nickel mining operation located near the city of Thompson in north-central Manitoba, Canada, within the Moak–Setting Lakes area along the Burntwood River. Discovered in 1956 by International Nickel Company, the deposit led directly to the creation of the planned mining city of Thompson. Mining began in 1961, and the operation became one of the first integrated nickel complexes in the Western Hemisphere. Today the mine is owned and operated by Vale Base Metals and remains a cornerstone employer in northern Manitoba, despite periodic restructuring and strategic reviews. Geologically, the Thompson Mine lies within the Thompson Nickel Belt, one of the world’s most important high-grade nickel sulfide districts. The belt sits along the boundary between the Archean Superior Craton and the Churchill Province, within the Circum-Superior Belt. Nickel mineralisation occurs in ultramafic rocks associated with complex folding, faulting, and metamorphism of the Ospwagan Group. The South Pit exposure is particularly notable for revealing overturned stratigraphy within a large plunging fold, making the area a classic site for structural and economic geology studies. Beyond geology and production, the Thompson Mine has faced significant social and environmental challenges. The closure of the local smelter and refinery resulted in job losses and increased reliance on shipping concentrate elsewhere. Workplace safety has been a major concern, highlighted by fatal accidents in 2011 and subsequent charges under Manitoba’s workplace safety laws. Indigenous rights and land use issues persist, as mining occurs within traditional territories. In 2025, Vale initiated a strategic review of its Thompson assets, creating uncertainty for the community even as exploration by new companies signals continued geological potential in the region.

The Canadian Malartic Mine is one of Canada’s largest gold mining operations, located approximately 25 kilometres west of Val-d’Or in northwestern Quebec, within the town of Malartic. Gold was first discovered in the area in 1923, triggering a major gold rush that helped establish Malartic as a mining town. Modern large-scale mining began in 2011 under Osisko Mining, and ownership transferred in 2014 to Agnico Eagle Mines and Yamana Gold (now Pan American Silver, with Agnico Eagle as operator). Today, the Canadian Malartic complex includes the Canadian Malartic open pit, the developing Odyssey underground mine, and the Marban project, forming a long-term regional mining hub. Open-pit operations are expected to continue until 2029, while the Odyssey Mine is projected to operate until at least 2042. Geologically, the mine lies within the Abitibi Gold Belt, one of the world’s most prolific Archean greenstone belts. Gold mineralisation is structurally controlled and hosted in volcanic and intrusive rocks affected by major fault systems. The deposit supports both large-scale open-pit mining at the Canadian Malartic and Barnat pits and deeper underground mining at Odyssey, which aims to access the down-plunge continuation of the orebody and significantly extend mine life. Despite its economic importance, the mine has been highly controversial due to its immediate proximity to the town of Malartic. Residents have reported persistent noise, dust, and vibrations, leading to health concerns, declining property values, and class-action lawsuits. Thousands of environmental infractions and perceived broken promises by operators have eroded community trust. Further opposition has emerged around the Odyssey expansion, which involves infrastructure relocation and neighborhood impacts, highlighting ongoing tensions between large-scale mining, environmental regulation, and community well-being.

site_location: Val-des-Sources, Les Sources RCM, Estrie, Québec
metadata: The Jeffrey Mine was a giant open-pit asbestos mine located at Val-des-Sources in southeastern Quebec, Canada. Mining began in 1879 and the pit ultimately grew to about 2 kilometres in diameter and 350 metres deep, making it one of the largest asbestos mines in the world. The mine dominated the town’s economy and physical layout for more than a century, at times requiring the relocation of entire neighbourhoods to allow pit expansion. After the Second World War, various underground methods were trialled, including block caving in the 1950s and later underground mining in the 1990s. However, global demand collapsed in the 1980s once asbestos was recognised as carcinogenic and banned in most countries. Despite government subsidies, operations ceased permanently in 2001. Geologically, the Jeffrey Mine exploited chrysotile asbestos hosted in ultramafic rocks of the Thetford–Ham area, part of a controversial ophiolitic complex in the Appalachian orogen. These ultramafic bodies are rich in serpentinised peridotite, within which chrysotile formed along fractures and shear zones. While traditionally interpreted as fragments of ancient oceanic crust (ophiolites), some researchers argue these rocks represent mantle-derived intrusions emplaced during Appalachian deformation, or even large submarine extrusions, reflecting ongoing debate about their origin. Beyond geology, the mine is deeply associated with labour, health, and political controversy. It was the site of the historic 1949 Asbestos Strike, a landmark event in Quebec labour history. Long-term health impacts on workers and residents became undeniable, shaping public opposition to asbestos mining. Failed restart attempts, environmental legacies, and stigma ultimately led the town to rename itself Val-des-Sources in 2020, symbolising a break from its asbestos past and a search for economic diversification.

The Guanaco–Amancaya Operation is a gold–silver mining district located in northern Chile, about 220 kilometres south-southeast of Antofagasta, within the municipality of Taltal. Owned 100 percent by Austral Gold, the operation includes multiple open-pit and underground mines at Guanaco and Amancaya, heap leach facilities, and satellite deposits such as Inesperada. Mining began in the district in 2010, building on earlier discoveries dating back to the late nineteenth century. Underground mining at Guanaco ceased in February 2020, followed by the closure of the Amancaya underground mine in late 2024. Current and future production is centred on heap leach reprocessing, launched in 2023, and the Inesperada open-pit project, which is expected to sustain operations into the mid-2020s. Since start-up, the district has produced approximately 485,000 ounces of gold and two million ounces of silver. Geologically, the Guanaco district lies within a Paleocene to early Eocene volcano-sedimentary basin dominated by volcanic, pyroclastic, and subvolcanic rocks of the Chile–Alemania Formation. Mineralisation is characteristic of a high-sulfidation epithermal system, structurally controlled by north-northeast and east-northeast fault systems. Gold and silver occur in ledge-type structures associated with phreatomagmatic breccias and dikes, particularly in the Dumbo area, which represents the core of the system, while other pits exploit satellite structures. Mineralisation formed during the Eocene and is hosted in deformed ignimbrite and epiclastic units. Additional issues surrounding the operation are relatively limited. The mine is located in an arid, sparsely populated area with low biodiversity and no permanent watercourses, reducing environmental and social risks. Nevertheless, the project is subject to Chile’s environmental assessment system and ongoing monitoring of air, water, flora, and fauna. Heap reprocessing and waste management remain key long-term considerations as the mine transitions toward closure, currently projected for around 2033.

The Tambo de Oro Mine is a gold operation in the Punitaqui mining district of the Coquimbo Region in north-central Chile, with a history closely tied to the broader development of the El Indio Gold Belt. Gold mining in the district expanded during the late twentieth century as modern exploration confirmed high-grade mineralisation along the Andes. Tambo became one of several significant mines in the belt, which also included El Indio, Pascua Lama, and other satellite operations. While some large mines in the belt were exhausted and closed by the early 2000s, smaller-scale operations such as Tambo de Oro continued, supported by evolving ownership and renewed investment. Operated by HMC Gold, a subsidiary of Haldeman Mining, the mine secured regulatory approval in December 2021 to extend operations by seven years and slightly increase production, ensuring its continued role in regional gold mining. Geologically, Tambo de Oro lies within the El Indio Gold Belt, a trans-Andean metallogenic province spanning Chile and Argentina and renowned for gold, silver, and copper mineralisation. The deposit consists of Early Cretaceous vein and breccia systems with mixed epithermal and mesothermal characteristics. More broadly, the belt is dominated by high-sulfidation epithermal deposits associated with volcanic and caldera-related hydrothermal systems. Alteration minerals such as alunite, barite, and enargite are common, and the area is notable for rare tellurium-bearing minerals. The nearby Wendy pit, part of the greater Tambo mine area, is internationally recognised as the type locality for several unique tellurite minerals. The mine has also been shaped by accidents and controversy. In September 2020, two miners were killed in a fatal rockfall, raising concerns about underground safety. In 2023, the mine was declared a “strategic enterprise” by Chile’s Ministry of the Interior, requiring private security measures; this designation has been challenged in court by the operator. These issues reflect wider tensions in the El Indio belt between continued mining, worker safety, regulatory control, and social acceptance.

site_location: Mantos de Punitaqui District, Punitaqui, Ovalle, Limarí Province, Coquimbo
metadata: The Dalmacia pit is part of the Punitaqui copper–gold–silver mining complex in the Coquimbo Region of north-central Chile and is now primarily an underground operation. The wider Punitaqui district has a long mining history, initially focused on gold and mercury before evolving into a significant copper producer. After years of intermittent activity, the complex was acquired in March 2021 by Battery Mineral Resources Corporation, which began a major redevelopment program. In May 2024, the company successfully restarted copper concentrate production at the Punitaqui processing plant, marking the return of the district as an active producer. Dalmacia is being advanced as a potential fourth underground mine, complementing the Cinabrio and San Andres mines and supporting long-term production growth. Geologically, the Dalmacia deposit differs from other Punitaqui orebodies. It is hosted within a roof pendant of Middle to Upper Jurassic volcano-sedimentary rocks intruded by younger granites. Mineralisation is classified as Iron Oxide Copper-Gold type and is strongly structurally controlled, occurring as veins, fracture fillings, and disseminations, particularly along contacts between granites and volcanic rocks. The main copper minerals are chalcopyrite and bornite, with minor pyrite, while near-surface zones contain oxide minerals such as chrysocolla, atacamite, and cuprite. Mineralised zones can reach widths of up to 50 metres and extend more than 500 metres vertically. Beyond geology, the project faces typical regional challenges rather than site-specific conflict. Restart efforts were initially slowed by permitting delays as legacy approvals were consolidated into a modern environmental framework. Water management remains sensitive in the semi-arid Coquimbo Region, and underground waste and tailings handling requires careful oversight. Although Dalmacia has avoided major protests, broader mining controversies in Coquimbo underscore ongoing social and environmental scrutiny.

metadata: The Shilu Mine in Yangchun County, Guangdong Province, China, is a historically important copper–molybdenum mining operation best known internationally for its exceptional mineral specimens rather than its industrial scale. Active for decades during the twentieth century, the mine became a classic locality for collectors, particularly during the 1990s, when spectacular azurite and malachite specimens entered the global mineral market. Industrial production focused mainly on copper, but declining ore quality, environmental constraints, and changing economic conditions eventually led to closure. By the mid-2020s, the main open-cast workings were documented as flooded, marking the end of active mining and a transition to a post-mining landscape. Geologically, the Shilu Mine is a skarn-type copper–molybdenum deposit developed within a karst environment hosted by Carboniferous carbonate strata. Mineralisation formed through hydrothermal interaction between intrusive bodies and limestone, producing copper-rich skarn assemblages. Extensive near-surface oxidation transformed primary sulfides into secondary copper minerals, making malachite the dominant ore mineral during much of the mine’s productive life. The site is world-renowned for its mineralogy, especially large azurite “roses” and velvety botryoidal malachite, often referred to as “Chinese mineral classics.” Other minerals include native copper, chrysocolla, and molybdenite, reflecting the polymetallic nature of the system. Beyond geology, the Shilu Mine is associated with several environmental and social concerns typical of older Chinese mining operations. Flooding of the open pit represents a major alteration of local karst hydrology and raises concerns about long-term groundwater contamination. Although site-specific data are limited, the mine lies within a region where heavy metal pollution of soils and water has been widely documented. The operation also dates from a period when mine safety and environmental regulation in China were comparatively weak. Finally, the site is often confused with the much larger Shilu Iron Mine in Hainan Province, an unrelated iron-oxide–copper–gold deposit, which complicates public understanding of its true scale and legacy.

The Kafang Mine is a major copper–tin operation located in the southern sector of the Gejiu polymetallic ore field in Yunnan Province, southwestern China. It is one of the most important copper producers in the Gejiu district, which is internationally recognised as a world-class tin province. While small-scale mining in the region dates back more than 2,000 years to the Han Dynasty, Kafang itself was developed through systematic modern exploration beginning in the 1970s. Today, the mine forms part of a mature but still active mining district that is increasingly focused on deeper and peripheral resources as shallow reserves become depleted. Geologically, the Kafang deposit is distinguished within the Gejiu district by its relatively high copper-to-tin ratio. Mineralisation is diverse and includes skarn-type copper–tin ores as well as stratiform copper ores hosted within Triassic basaltic volcanic rocks and associated carbonate sequences. The deposit reflects a complex hydrothermal system involving interaction between magmatic fluids and carbonate host rocks. Although historically debated, recent geochemical and isotopic studies increasingly support a granite-related hydrothermal origin rather than a purely sedimentary-exhalative model, helping to clarify long-standing questions about ore formation in the district. Despite its economic significance, the Kafang Mine and the wider Gejiu area are marked by serious environmental and social challenges. Studies published up to 2025 document severe heavy metal contamination of soils and groundwater, particularly arsenic, lead, cadmium, and zinc, posing ongoing health risks. Illegal and informal mining has caused ground instability and structural collapses in nearby communities. The decline of mining activity has also been linked to social distress, including poverty, crime, and public health crises. Together, these issues highlight the complex legacy of intensive polymetallic mining in one of China’s most historically important mineral districts.

site_location: Královské Poříčí, Sokolov District, Karlovy Vary Region
metadata: The Jiří Open Pit Mine is the largest and one of the last two active brown coal (lignite) surface mines in the Sokolov Basin of western Czech Republic. Located near Chodov in the Alberov mining region, it is owned and operated by Sokolovská uhelná and has long been a cornerstone of the local economy. The mine supplies coal both to domestic and foreign customers and to the company’s own power plants, notably Tisová. Although production has fluctuated in recent years, it has typically ranged between about 3 and 4 million tonnes annually. Mining is expected to continue into the early 2030s, after which the pit will be reclaimed and flooded to form the planned Jiří–Družba lake around 2040. Geologically, the Jiří mine exploits thick lignite seams within the Sokolov Basin, a Tertiary sedimentary basin known for its extensive brown coal deposits. The mine primarily targets the Antonín seam, one of the thickest and most economically important coal seams in the region. As with other open-pit coal operations, extraction requires the removal of large volumes of overburden, fundamentally reshaping the landscape and altering local hydrogeology. The Jiří mine is also associated with significant environmental and social controversy. Open-pit mining has transformed large areas into spoil heaps and degraded landscapes, while concerns persist about groundwater disruption and long-term water quality, particularly given sensitivities around protected spa springs in the wider region. Community fears of village displacement echo the historic destruction of dozens of settlements in other Czech coal districts. The mine has also become a focal point in debates over climate change and energy transition. Although Sokolovská uhelná planned to phase out coal earlier, the energy crisis following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine delayed closures. In line with updated national and European Union climate commitments, coal mining is now scheduled to end nationwide by 2033, with controlled downsizing and job losses already underway at Jiří.

The Jezernice clay pit, historically known as a hliniště, is a former clay extraction site located near the village of Jezernice in the Přerov District of the Olomouc Region, Czech Republic. The pit was developed to supply high-quality loess and brick clay for local construction and brickmaking, supporting rural building activity in the region for generations. Jezernice itself is a small settlement best known for its historic railway viaducts, protected as cultural monuments, and the fifteenth-century Church of Saint Martin, which together reflect the area’s long interaction between infrastructure, landscape, and settlement. Geologically, the Jezernice pit is situated within the Moravian Gate, a lowland corridor filled with Quaternary loess, clays, and fine-grained sediments deposited by wind and water. These deposits are well suited for brick production due to their plasticity and uniform grain size. Similar clay pits across the Přerov region have also proven archaeologically significant, as the same clay-rich sediments were exploited by prehistoric communities. Excavations nearby have revealed settlement features and extraction pits dating back to the Bronze Age, around the fifteenth century before Christ, demonstrating long-term human use of local clay resources for pottery and construction. Although the Jezernice clay pit itself is not associated with major contemporary controversies, it lies within an environmentally sensitive region. The wider district has faced serious groundwater risks, highlighted in 2025 by a major benzene spill following a railway accident in nearby Hustopeče nad Bečvou. The Moravian Gate is also prone to severe flooding, which can remobilize pollutants from abandoned industrial and extraction sites. Like many former clay pits in the Czech Republic, Jezernice faces ongoing challenges related to reclamation, monitoring, and preventing illegal dumping, while also offering potential for ecological restoration or small-scale cultural and educational use.

The Horní Bříza kaolin pit is one of the most important and long-standing industrial mining sites in the Czech Republic, supplying high-quality kaolin to domestic and international ceramics and paper industries. Located about 8 kilometres north of Plzeň, the open-cast mine has been in continuous operation since 1877 and is currently operated by LB MINERALS, part of the Lasselsberger Group. Together with the nearby Kaznějov pit, Horní Bříza accounts for roughly 80 percent of the country’s crude kaolin production, making it a cornerstone of the regional and national mining economy. The mine has shaped the development of the town of Horní Bříza, which grew alongside kaolin processing facilities and ceramic manufacturing from the late nineteenth century onward. Geologically, the deposit consists of primary kaolin formed through deep chemical weathering of Carboniferous arkoses, which are feldspar-rich sandstones. Kaolinization penetrates to depths of approximately 80 to 110 metres, producing a high-purity clay suitable for fine ceramics, paper coating, and fillers. This type of primary kaolin differs from sedimentary deposits in that the clay remains largely in place over its parent rock, allowing large-scale open-pit extraction. Despite its economic value, the mine is associated with environmental and social tensions typical of open-cast operations. Landscape transformation, deforestation, dust, and noise affect surrounding communities, while research has shown that kaolin spoil substrates are extremely nutrient-poor, reducing soil biological activity. At the same time, abandoned sections of the pit have become unexpected refuges for rare insects and spiders adapted to open, early-successional habitats, creating debate over reclamation strategies. As mining continues into the mid-twenty-first century, discussions focus on balancing industrial production, ecological restoration, and the long-term future of the Horní Bříza landscape.

site_location: Březno, Chomutov District, Ústí nad Labem Region
metadata: The Libouš Quarry is a large, active open-pit lignite (brown coal) mine located near Březno in the Chomutov District of north-western Czech Republic. It forms part of the Nástup–Tušimice mining complex within the North Bohemian Lignite Basin and has been in continuous large-scale operation since 1964. The mine’s expansion led to profound social changes: the historic village of Libouš was completely demolished and officially abolished in 1969 to allow the quarry to advance, a fate shared by many settlements in the region during the socialist era of intensive energy extraction. Today, Libouš remains a key pillar of Czech energy security, supplying lignite primarily to the Tušimice II and Prunéřov II power plants. Geologically, the quarry exploits Miocene-aged coal seams of the Most Formation, particularly the Holešice Member, overlain by lacustrine clays and silts. These sediments are notable not only for their economic lignite resources but also for their mineralogical and scientific importance. Libouš is the type locality for rare ammonium-bearing minerals such as ferrierite-NH₄ and tschermigite, and the clays have yielded Early Miocene plant fossils that document a subtropical to temperate forest environment around 17 million years ago. Despite frequent public discussion about the end of coal, Libouš remains active as of late 2025, producing on the order of 10–14 million tonnes of lignite annually using massive bucket-wheel excavators. Current national policy foresees a coal exit by around 2033, and Libouš is expected to wind down in the early 2030s. Environmental controversies focus on the mine’s enormous landscape footprint, long-term groundwater disruption, and the unresolved social legacy of displaced communities. Planned post-mining hydric reclamation would transform the pit into a large artificial lake, but critics question whether such projects can truly compensate for decades of ecological and cultural loss.

The Shangulowé Mine is a historic open-cast copper–cobalt mine located about 10 kilometers northwest of Kambove in Haut-Katanga Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo. It forms part of the renowned Congolese Copperbelt and was historically a satellite operation within the larger Kambove mining complex. Developed under Belgian colonial rule by Union Minière du Haut-Katanga, the mine later passed to the state-owned company Gécamines. Large-scale industrial mining has ceased, and Shangulowé is now considered an exhausted locality: production has ended, but mineral specimens from the site remain highly prized by collectors and museums. Geologically, Shangulowé is hosted in the Roan Group of the Katanga Supergroup. The deposit is famous for its well-developed supergene enrichment zone, where primary copper–cobalt sulfides were oxidized near the surface. This process produced exceptional secondary minerals, including world-class baryte crystal clusters, often tabular or chisel-shaped and sometimes tinted by malachite. The mine is also notable for copper silicates such as planchéite and shattuckite, including rare pseudomorphs where baryte has been completely replaced by these minerals. Despite its closure, Shangulowé remains embedded in the broader environmental and social challenges of the Kambove region. Artisanal miners frequently rework old pits and tailings under hazardous, unregulated conditions. Legacy pollution from open-cast mining contributes to acid runoff and heavy-metal contamination affecting nearby water bodies and agriculture. In parallel, modern copper–cobalt production in the area is now dominated by China Nonferrous Metal Mining Group, a major state-owned enterprise operating nearby assets. While the company promotes “green mining,” social projects, and compliance with international environmental standards, critics describe these initiatives as greenwashing, noting the persistent ecological damage, ongoing artisanal mining, and unresolved questions about long-term environmental rehabilitation and supply-chain accountability.

The Mindingi Mine is a copper- and cobalt-producing mine located in the Kambove Territory of Haut-Katanga Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo, within the mineral-rich Katangese Copperbelt.Historically exploited for copper and cobalt, the site today is less notable for industrial production than for its role in illustrating the environmental and socio-economic consequences of mining in a region where agriculture and extractive industries coexist uneasily. The mine lies in the Basanga chiefdom along National Road Number One between Likasi and Kolwezi, an area of both high farming productivity and intense mining pressure. Geologically, the Mindingi deposit is hosted in the Neoproterozoic Katanga Supergroup, which contains some of the world’s most significant copper–cobalt mineralization. Like nearby deposits, it features oxidized and supergene zones rich in secondary copper minerals.. Beyond geology, Mindingi has become a focal point for environmental and social controversy. Mining effluents from the broader Kambove area, including Mindingi and nearby sites, have repeatedly contaminated the Mulungwishi River and its irrigated valleys. Farmers report discolored, foul-smelling water, widespread crop failure, fish deaths, and loss of livestock, particularly during severe pollution events documented around 2013–2014. Studies and local testimony indicate that more than 2,500 farming households have been affected. Despite a joint impact assessment involving the state-owned mining company Gécamines, efforts to secure compensation have largely failed. Persistent heavy-metal contamination, including cobalt, copper, and uranium, also raises serious long-term public health concerns, making Mindingi a prominent case study of mining-related environmental injustice in the Congolese Copperbelt.

The Kansoko Mine is an underground copper mine forming a key component of the Kamoa–Kakula copper project, a globally significant mining development in Lualaba Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo. Located near the town of Mutshatsha, approximately 25 kilometers west of Kolwezi, Kansoko is planned as a major production center within the broader Kamoa–Kakula complex, complementing the flagship Kakula deposit. The project is a joint venture between Ivanhoe Mines, Zijin Mining Group Co. Ltd., and the government of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and has been ranked by Wood Mackenzie as the world’s largest undeveloped high-grade copper discovery. Kansoko lies within the Central African Copperbelt, specifically the Katangese Copper Crescent, one of the most prolific copper and cobalt provinces on Earth. Mineralization is hosted in the lower units of the Neoproterozoic Katanga Supergroup, where stratiform copper deposits extend laterally over large areas and occur at relatively shallow depths. Copper is the primary economic metal, with cobalt as a significant by-product. The surrounding geological environment is also enriched in trace elements such as uranium, cadmium, lead, manganese, and nickel, which can be mobilized during mining and processing. The local landscape consists of mineralized hills, plateaus, and clay-sandy valley soils that are particularly sensitive to disturbance. The Kansoko area reflects wider transformations across Lualaba Province, where copper and cobalt mining now account for nearly 20 percent of the national economy. Since the early 2000s, control of many former state-owned assets has shifted to foreign operators, including Chinese companies, reshaping the regional mining economy. Mining activities have led to extensive land reshaping, water contamination, and agricultural losses, with rivers showing abnormal coloration linked to cobalt saturation and chemical neutralization processes. Local farming communities report crop failures, fish deaths, and long-term health risks from heavy-metal exposure. Efforts to seek compensation are often hindered by lack of formal land titles and the legal and political power of mining companies, underscoring ongoing conflicts between global demand for critical metals and local livelihoods.

The Mashamba West Mine is an open-cast copper–cobalt mine located in Mutshatsha, Lualaba Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo, about 11 kilometers southwest of Kolwezi. Mining began in 1978 under the state-owned company Gécamines, but the mine’s modern trajectory has been strongly shaped by its integration into the Sicomines (Sino-Congolaise des Mines) project, a large joint venture dominated by a consortium of Chinese state-owned companies. Through this arrangement, Mashamba West became part of a strategic, China-backed mining portfolio tied to long-term access to copper and cobalt in exchange for infrastructure financing, reflecting the growing influence of Chinese capital and industrial planning in the Congolese Copperbelt. Mashamba West lies within the Kolwezi Klippe of the Katanga Copper Crescent, one of the world’s richest sediment-hosted copper–cobalt provinces. Mineralization occurs in Neoproterozoic sedimentary rocks of the Katanga Supergroup, specifically the Mines Group, where stratiform copper sulfides were extensively oxidized. This process produced an exceptional assemblage of secondary minerals, making the mine a classic locality for cobalt-bearing calcite, vivid red cuprite, baryte, malachite, spherocobaltite, kolwezite, vesigniéite, and uranium minerals such as carnotite and metatyuyamunite. Mashamba West is widely regarded as far more mineralogically significant than the nearby Mashamba East pit. Under the Sicomines framework, large-scale open-pit mining has intensified environmental pressures in Mutshatsha. Deforestation of Miombo woodlands, soil degradation, and contamination of rivers with copper, cobalt, and uranium are persistent concerns. Local communities report crop failures, polluted water, and health risks linked to heavy metals. Although the Chinese-led consortium highlights infrastructure development and economic benefits, critics argue that land expropriation, limited transparency, and inadequate compensation for displaced or affected residents remain unresolved sources of tension.

The Limberg Quarry, together with the adjacent Skräbböle Quarry, forms the core of the Pargas (Parainen) limestone mining complex in Southwest Finland. These formerly separate open pits have gradually merged into a single vast open-cast mine approximately 1–2 kilometers long and about 0.5 kilometers wide, making it the largest open-pit mine in Finland. Operated by Nordkalk, the quarry has been central to the economy of Pargas for centuries and remains the municipality’s most important industrial activity, supplying limestone and lime products to domestic and European markets. The quarry exploits a high-grade Proterozoic marble deposit within the Fennoscandian Shield. This marble represents recrystallized limestone, dominated by high-purity calcite and enclosed by gneissic host rocks. The deposit is steeply dipping and structurally complex due to ancient folding and metamorphism. Limberg is internationally renowned in mineralogy as the type locality for pargasite, a magnesium-rich amphibole named after Pargas, and it has yielded more than 80 identified mineral species, including chondrodite and other rare silicates. The exceptional mineral diversity reflects the interaction of carbonate rocks with silica- and magnesium-rich fluids during metamorphism. The quarry produces limestone for quicklime, slaked lime, aggregates, and industrial fillers used in steelmaking, construction, agriculture, water treatment, and chemical industries. Annual production has exceeded one million tonnes in peak years, making Nordkalk a dominant local employer and economic actor. However, the scale of extraction has permanently transformed the landscape, creating a pit extending well below sea level. Environmental concerns focus on visual impact, groundwater management, noise, and dust, particularly given Pargas’s location within the sensitive Archipelago Sea region. While mining underpins local livelihoods and exports, the municipality’s economic dependence on limestone also raises long-term questions about diversification, reclamation, and the future use of the enormous quarry once extraction eventually ceases.

site_location: Tiébaghi Massif, Koumac, Northern Province, New Caledonia
metadata: The Tiébaghi Mine, which historically extracted chromite (chromium ore) and today exploits nickel laterite ores, is located on the Tiébaghi Massif near Koumac in the Northern Province of New Caledonia and ranks among the most significant mining sites in the Pacific. Chromite was first discovered there in 1877, with large-scale mining beginning in 1902. Initially worked as an open pit, the mine shifted to underground operations from 1927 to 1962, briefly reopened between 1982 and 1990, and over its lifetime produced approximately 3.27 million tonnes of chromite ore, accounting for about 87 percent of New Caledonia’s total chromium production. At its peak in the mid-20th century, Tiébaghi was the world’s largest and richest chromium mine, with ore grades reaching up to 56 percent chromium oxide. Geologically, the mine is hosted within the Tiébaghi ultramafic massif, an ophiolite composed mainly of peridotite representing obducted oceanic mantle. Chromite occurs in podiform ore bodies aligned northwest–southeast and steeply dipping within dunitic layers of the peridotite. Prolonged tropical weathering of these ultramafic rocks subsequently concentrated nickel into lateritic and saprolitic horizons, forming the basis of current operations. The mine’s history also includes industrial hazards and social conflict, notably a fatal dynamite explosion in 1951 that killed two young engineers. Chromium mining ceased definitively in the early 1990s due to declining grades and high costs. Today, Société Le Nickel, a subsidiary of Eramet, operates large-scale open-cast nickel mining on the massif. While economically vital, these activities have caused extensive landscape disturbance, erosion, and ecological stress, contributing to the endangerment of endemic species such as the New Caledonian marbled gecko. Alongside active mining, parts of Tiébaghi are preserved as an industrial heritage site, highlighting both its global mineralogical importance and its complex environmental legacy.

The Salsigne Gold Mine located in the Orbiel Valley about 15 kilometers north of Carcassonne in southern France, is one of the country’s most significant and controversial mining sites. Active in various forms since Roman times, the district historically produced iron, copper, lead, and small amounts of gold. Modern gold mining began in 1892 after the discovery of gold-bearing ore by Louis Marius Esparseil. From 1892 until its final closure in 2004, Salsigne became the largest gold mine in Western Europe, producing approximately 120 tonnes of gold, 300 tonnes of silver, and making France the world’s leading producer of arsenic during the twentieth century. Geologically, the deposit lies on the southern flank of the Montagne Noire and consists of a complex hydrothermal system hosted in Paleozoic sedimentary rocks, including limestones and sandstones. The primary ore mineral was arsenopyrite, which carried gold intimately bound to arsenic and sulfur. This mineralogical complexity required intensive processing, including cyanide-based hydrometallurgy and pyrometallurgy, generating vast quantities of toxic waste rich in arsenic, mercury, lead, and cadmium. The mine’s legacy is dominated by its environmental and public health impacts. Millions of tonnes of arsenic-rich tailings were left in the Orbiel Valley, contaminating soils and waterways. Studies estimate that between 3 and 8 tonnes of arsenic are still transported annually by the Orbiel River. Floods in 2018 redistributed contaminated sediments, intensifying exposure risks. Subsequent health surveys revealed elevated arsenic levels in local children, leading authorities to ban the sale of vegetables and restrict land use. Despite extensive remediation efforts by French state agencies, Salsigne remains emblematic of the long-term environmental costs of metal mining and continues to be one of France’s most severe industrial pollution cases

site_location: Decazeville coal basin, Decazeville, Villefranche-de-Rouergue, Aveyron, Occitanie
metadata: La Découverte de Lassalle is a large former open-pit coal mine located in Decazeville, in the Aveyron department of southern France. Opened in 1892 and operated until 2001, it exploited bituminous coal and ranks among the largest open-cast mines ever developed in France, measuring approximately 3.7 kilometers in length and 2.5 kilometers in width. The mine is part of the historic Decazeville–Firmi coal basin, a cornerstone of regional industrialization from the nineteenth century onward. Its iconic central headframe, now protected as a historic monument since 2019, remains a prominent landmark of the town. Geologically, the mine lies within the Carboniferous Decazeville Basin, composed of cyclic sedimentary units known as assises. The Lassalle pit primarily exploited the Bourran cycle, made up of conglomerates, sandstones, shales, and coal seams that were strongly folded and faulted by tectonic compression. As open-cast mining deepened, it intersected and replaced older underground workings, which were progressively abandoned after 1965 following national coal policies aimed at reducing production. After closure, the site entered a long and technically complex rehabilitation phase. Between 2001 and 2004, authorities implemented an after-mine management strategy focused on groundwater control and ground stability. The pit was intentionally flooded to form a managed lake, now around 25–40 meters deep, maintained at a carefully selected elevation to prevent destabilization of nearby shallow underground galleries. Continuous pumping from the central shaft is required to regulate water levels and protect surrounding districts.
