Business and Human Rights Resource Centre reported investigations alleging poor working conditions at Kamoto Copper Company, including concerns raised by employees and contractors; Glencore has previously issued comments disputing or responding to such claims. The mine has also faced operational setbacks, weak cobalt prices, and royalty disputes. In 2025, Mining Technology reported that Glencore was exploring a sale of a majority stake in Kamoto Copper Company, and later reporting described talks involving a United States-backed consortium for a stake in Glencore’s Democratic Republic of the Congo copper and cobalt assets.

Sentinel Mine is a large open-pit copper mine in Kalumbila District, North-Western Province, Zambia, extracting mainly copper, with associated nickel and cobalt mineralisation in the wider Trident project area. It is operated by First Quantum Minerals through First Quantum Minerals Trident Limited. The mine is about 150 kilometres west of Solwezi and is part of the Trident complex, which also includes the Enterprise nickel mine. First Quantum reports copper capacity of 300,000 metric tonnes per year, about 7,000 jobs created, and 754 million metric tonnes of measured and indicated mineral resources. Geologically, Sentinel lies at the western end of the Lufilian Arc, in Zambia’s “new copper belt.” The deposit is sediment-hosted and stratabound, occurring mainly in strongly deformed, carbonaceous metapelitic rocks known as Kalumbila phyllite. Copper mineralisation is concentrated in this phyllite unit, with the ore body striking roughly east–west for about 11 kilometres and dipping 20 to 30 degrees northward. Chalcopyrite is the main copper-bearing mineral, commonly occurring in millimetre-scale quartz–kyanite–carbonate veinlets parallel to bedding and foliation. Near-surface oxidation extends to about 70 metres depth, with chalcocite and tarnished chalcopyrite, while the upper 5 to 15 metres is commonly leached or contains mixed refractory copper and trace oxide minerals. Additional issues include land, livelihood and resettlement controversies. In 2025, communities near the Kansanshi and Sentinel mines began legal action alleging that First Quantum Minerals’ mine expansion caused involuntary resettlement, loss of farming land, inadequate compensation, fenced-off customary land and increased hardship. The claims remain allegations in pre-action legal correspondence, but they highlight continuing social-risk concerns around large-scale copper mining in the area.

Lumwana Mine is a large open-pit copper mine in Kalumbila District, North-Western Province, Zambia, extracting mainly copper from sulphide ore and producing copper concentrate. It is operated by Barrick Mining Corporation through Lumwana Mining Company. The mine lies about 100 kilometres west of Solwezi and includes the Malundwe and Chimiwungo deposits, with an expansion project intended to turn Lumwana into a longer-life, higher-output copper operation. Barrick describes Lumwana as a conventional truck-and-shovel open-pit mine using sulphide flotation, and recent project reporting says the expansion is designed to raise average copper output to about 240,000 metric tonnes per year and extend mine life to 2057. Geologically, Lumwana is part of the Central African Copperbelt and is hosted in the Mwombezhi Dome of the Lufilian Arc. Mineralisation occurs mainly in strongly deformed, sulphide-bearing schists and gneisses. The principal ore bodies, Malundwe and Chimiwungo, are broadly similar, with mineralised schist units bounded by hanging-wall and footwall gneisses. At Malundwe, the mineralised schist strikes roughly north–south, dips about 10 to 15 degrees west, and plunges gently south. The ore is predominantly sulphide, with copper minerals processed into concentrate. Additional issues include major expansion impacts, labour and environmental health concerns. Construction of the approximately 1.8 billion euro super-pit expansion was reported to be under way in 2025, raising questions about land use, water management, tailings and long-term closure planning. A controversy also concerns alleged uranium exposure: Business and Human Rights Resource Centre reported that Barrick acknowledged uranium had been detected in drinking water in one mine area and said exposure was limited and negligible; more than 190 former workers later reportedly sued Lumwana Mining Company over alleged exposure, claims the company has disputed.

Lefa Mine is a large open-pit gold mine in Guinea, extracting gold as its principal mineral. It is operated by Nordgold and is located near Lero, about 680 kilometres from Conakry, in the Siguiri Prefecture of Kankan Region. The Lefa mine complex consists of multiple pits and deposits, including Lero, Fayalala, Banko and other satellite areas, within a concession area reported by Mindat as about 2,550 square kilometres. Nordgold acquired the mine in 2010 and describes it as one of Guinea’s leading gold producers, supporting more than 1,300 direct jobs and more than 1,000 indirect jobs. Geologically, Lefa lies in the Siguiri Basin, part of the Paleoproterozoic Birimian terrain of the West African Craton. The host rocks are mainly volcanosedimentary greenstone-belt rocks, metamorphosed to greenschist facies or higher and intruded by felsic plutons. Mining Data Online describes the deposit style as breccia-pipe, stockwork and narrow-vein mineralisation. Regionally, Siguiri gold deposits are orogenic systems, with gold commonly associated with quartz veins, fractures, fault zones and hydrothermal remobilisation in deformed metasedimentary and metavolcanic rocks. Additional issues include labour disputes, community impacts and environmental concerns. In March 2023, Reuters reported that Russia was working with Guinea to resolve a labour dispute at Lefa that had disrupted production at a mine producing about 8 metric tonnes of gold in 2022. Environmental justice reporting has raised concerns over cyanide use, land disturbance, water pollution, community displacement and demands for relocation or compensation near affected villages.

Sadiola Gold Mine is an open-pit gold mine near Sadiola in the Kayes Region of western Mali, close to the Senegal border. It extracts gold from oxide, transitional and sulphide ore and is operated by Société d’Exploitation des Mines d’Or de Sadiola. Current ownership is reported as Allied Gold Corporation at 80 percent and the Republic of Mali at 20 percent. The mine began commercial production in 1996 and is undergoing expansion intended to extend its operating life and increase output. Geologically, Sadiola lies in the Kéniéba–Kédougou window of the West African Craton, within Paleoproterozoic Birimian rocks. The district contains volcanic and sedimentary sequences, with the mine hosted mainly in the Kofi Formation near a regional structural corridor. Gold mineralisation is associated with alteration, quartz veining, brecciation and sulphide-bearing zones, and includes deeper fresh-rock sulphide material below the historically mined oxide zones. Mining Technology describes the operation as including a main open pit and satellite pits across a remote concession area of about 302 square kilometres. Additional issues include security, governance and human-rights concerns. In May 2025, a convoy carrying heavy mining equipment from Bamako to Sadiola was attacked in Kayes Region, underscoring wider security risks for mining in Mali and the Sahel. Allied Gold also reached a 10-year permit and tax-related agreement with Mali’s military government, amid broader pressure on foreign miners under the country’s revised mining code. Evidencity has flagged human-rights risks linked to gold mining in Mali, including state involvement, security forces and the vulnerability of local communities near industrial mines.

Bissa Mine is an active open-pit gold mine in Bam Province, Burkina Faso, extracting gold as its main mineral. It forms part of the Bissa–Bouly operation, with Bouly located about 5 kilometres from Bissa, and is operated by Bissa Gold, a Nordgold subsidiary. Nordgold owns 90 percent of the operation, while the government of Burkina Faso owns 10 percent. Bissa began production in January 2013 and uses drilling, blasting, loading and hauling, with ore treated in a 4.8 million metric tonnes per year carbon-in-leach plant; Bouly ore is processed through heap leaching and final treatment at Bissa. Geologically, Bissa is an orogenic gold deposit in the Boromo greenstone belt. The deposits occur within an approximately 90-kilometre northwest–southeast zone of Birimian volcano-sedimentary rocks, including mafic volcanic rocks, minor felsic volcanic rocks and extensive sedimentary units intruded by Eburnean granitoids. Mineralisation is structurally controlled and hosted mainly in metasedimentary rocks within brittle–ductile shear zones. Gold occurs in quartz–sulphide and quartz–carbonate veins, with pyrite as the dominant sulphide and minor chalcopyrite, arsenopyrite and rare native gold. Oxidation commonly includes a surface laterite layer up to 20 metres thick, above saprolite and transitional zones, with fresh rock typically beginning 50 to 125 metres below surface. Additional issues include displacement, compensation disputes and security risk. Environmental justice reporting says several thousand villagers, farmers and artisanal miners were displaced during mine construction, with complaints about compensation and loss of farmland. Nordgold has publicised community support, including a 100 metric tonne corn donation during food insecurity. Wider controversy includes scrutiny of Nordgold’s Russian ownership, sanctions affecting production disclosure, Burkina Faso’s denial that it favoured Nordgold in permitting, and regional insecurity that led Nordgold to shut its Taparko mine in 2022.

Ahafo Mine is a major gold-mining complex in Ghana’s Ahafo Region, extracting gold as its principal mineral from the Ahafo South and Ahafo North operations. Ahafo South is located about 290 kilometres northwest of Accra and has produced more than 311,000 kilograms of gold since first production in 2006. Newmont operates the mine with a 90 percent interest, while the Government of Ghana holds a 10 percent free-carried interest. The complex includes surface and underground mining, and Newmont says the addition of Ahafo North, which reached commercial production in October 2025, is expected to lift combined district production to about 23,300 kilograms of gold per year. Geologically, Ahafo South lies in the Sefwi greenstone belt of the Birimian Supergroup, within the West African Craton. The main deposits include Subika, Awonsu, Apensu and Amoma. Gold mineralisation is structurally controlled and hosted in deformed volcanic, sedimentary and intrusive rocks, with higher grades commonly associated with quartz–calcite veins, pyrite and cataclasite zones. At Subika, mineralisation extends below the former open pit into an underground deposit tested to about 1,600 metres vertical depth, while Awonsu has been drilled to about 600 metres depth. Additional issues include fiscal, environmental and social concerns. Reuters reported in 2025 that Newmont’s chief executive officer stressed the importance of stable taxes and royalties as the company opened the 900 million United States dollar Ahafo North mine. Ghana has since signalled possible changes to mining stability agreements, creating uncertainty for large investors. Operational concerns include water management, as Ahafo South stores excess contact water in the mined-out Apensu pit, while broader community issues around land access, resettlement, employment and benefit-sharing remain important for the district.

Buzwagi Gold Mine is a closed open-pit mine near Kahama in Shinyanga Region, northwestern Tanzania, that extracted mainly gold, with copper as an associated by-product. It is located about 6 kilometres southeast of Kahama and formed part of Barrick’s former Tanzanian mine portfolio, later managed through Twiga Minerals Corporation. Mining began in May 2009, the operation produced its last gold in 2021, and the mine was officially closed in July 2022. At peak, Mining Technology describes it as Tanzania’s second-largest operating mine and largest single open-pit mine; its processing plant could treat about 12,000 metric tonnes of ore per day. Geologically, Buzwagi lies in the Lake Victoria Goldfields, within the Nzega greenstone belt of the Nyanzian system. The deposit is described as a shear-hosted, quartz-veined gold deposit hosted mainly in porphyritic granite intruding basaltic and intermediate volcanic rocks. Mineralisation is structurally controlled, with gold associated with quartz veins and altered granitic host rocks. Mindat lists Buzwagi as a significant commercial gold deposit. Additional issues include closure, economic transition and wider controversies around Barrick’s Tanzanian operations. Barrick has promoted Buzwagi’s post-mining conversion into a training academy and special economic zone; the academy is intended to train more than 2,000 supervisors and managers, while the zone is projected to support at least 3,000 jobs per year. However, the mine’s history also included operational controversy, including reported fuel theft suspensions in 2010, and broader scrutiny of Barrick in Tanzania over human-rights, policing and community impacts, particularly at North Mara rather than Buzwagi specifically.

Carajás Mine is a vast open-pit mine complex in the Carajás Mountains of Pará, northern Brazil, extracting mainly high-grade iron ore, with the wider mineral province also containing manganese, copper, nickel, gold and bauxite. Operated by Vale, it is widely described as the world’s largest open-pit iron ore mine. NASA reports that the mine contains more than 7 billion metric tonnes of iron ore and is connected by rail to the Atlantic port of São Luís, more than 250 kilometres away. Geologically, Carajás lies in the Carajás Mineral Province on the southeastern Amazonian Craton. Its iron ore is hosted mainly in Precambrian banded iron formations, especially jaspilite units of the approximately 2.7-billion-year-old Grão Pará Group, a metamorphosed volcanic-sedimentary sequence with basalts above and below the iron formations. The Serra Norte deposits include N4E, N4W, N5E and N5S, where high-grade hematite-rich ore formed through structural control and hydrothermal upgrading of earlier iron-rich rocks. The province is also a major source of copper, nickel, manganese and gold. Additional issues include deforestation, infrastructure expansion, land conflict and Indigenous and agrarian-community impacts in the Amazon. NASA notes that scientists study Carajás partly to understand how mining affects Amazon deforestation. Critics describe the broader Carajás Project as rooted in Brazil’s military-dictatorship-era development model, with long-running concerns over land, labour and unequal benefits. Recent reporting by the Pulitzer Center found dozens of mining requests for copper, manganese and nickel affecting land-reform settlements in the Carajás region, raising fears of contaminated waterways and declining fish populations.

Aurizona Gold Mine is an open-pit gold mine near Godofredo Viana in Maranhão, northeastern Brazil, extracting gold as its main mineral. The mine is close to an Atlantic Ocean inlet and lies between São Luís and Belém. Mindat records Aurizona as a gold mine operated by Luna Gold from 2010 to 2015 and by Equinox Gold from 2016 onward; artisanal gold production in the area is reported to date back to the seventeenth century. Mining Technology describes an expansion plan intended to extend the mine life to about 11 years, with average annual production of about 4,260 kilograms of gold. Geologically, Aurizona is in the São Luís Craton and Gurupi Belt region, locally overlain by Neoproterozoic to Cambrian sedimentary rocks and cut by early Cambrian dykes. The mine is commonly described as a greenstone-hosted orogenic gold system. Its main deposits include Piaba, Boa Esperança, Tatajuba, Genipapo and Touro. Gold mineralisation is associated with shear zones, quartz veins, alteration zones and structurally controlled mineralised corridors in Paleoproterozoic rocks. Additional issues include environmental risk, water contamination concerns and community conflict. Business and Human Rights Resource Centre reported allegations from the Movement of Dam Affected People that a dam in the Aurizona community broke on 25 March 2021 and that tailings contaminated the Tromaí River; the page includes responses from Mineração Aurizona and Equinox Gold. Earthworks later criticised Equinox Gold for allegedly downplaying the 2021 failure, saying the incident compromised water access for nearly 4,000 people. Other local reports have alleged land disturbance, intimidation and continuing tensions between residents and the mining company; the company has denied or disputed some claims.

Salobo Mine is a large open-pit copper and gold mine in Pará, northern Brazil, extracting mainly copper, with gold and silver as associated precious metals. It is operated by Vale in the Carajás region, and Wheaton Precious Metals describes it as the largest copper deposit discovered in Brazil. The mine began operating in 2012 with a processing capacity of 12 million metric tonnes per year and is being expanded toward 36 million metric tonnes per year. Geologically, Salobo is an iron oxide–copper–gold deposit in the Carajás Mining District, within the Archean Carajás Province of the southeastern Amazonian Craton. The deposit is hosted in a west-northwest to east-southeast trending basin and is associated with intense iron-rich and potassium-rich hydrothermal alteration. The main ore minerals include chalcopyrite and bornite, with gold and silver occurring as by-products. Mineralisation is structurally controlled and hosted in altered, mylonitic rocks rich in biotite, garnet, quartz, magnetite, actinolite, grunerite and tourmaline. Deep exploration beneath the planned open pit has investigated mineralisation to about 700 metres depth over about 1,800 metres of strike length. Additional issues include environmental justice concerns, land-use conflict and revenue disputes. Environmental justice reporting links copper mining in Carajás, including Salobo and Sossego, to impacts on forest areas, communities, water resources and protected landscapes in Pará. Salobo is reported to operate in the Tapirapé-Aquiri National Forest area, increasing scrutiny of mining inside sensitive Amazonian territories. A Brazilian Parliamentary Inquiry Commission also alleged that Vale did not properly declare gold and silver extracted from Salobo and therefore failed to pay the full Financial Compensation for Mineral Exploration owed to public authorities.

Chapada Mine is a large open-pit copper and gold mine in northern Goiás State, Brazil, extracting copper and gold and producing a gold-rich copper concentrate. It is wholly owned by Lundin Mining and operated by its Brazilian subsidiary, Mineração Maracá Indústria e Comércio Sociedade Anônima. The mine is about 320 kilometres north of Goiânia and 270 kilometres northwest of Brasília. Lundin reports that the processing plant treats about 65,000 metric tonnes of ore per day and that concentrate is trucked to the port of Açu for export. Geologically, Chapada includes the Chapada and Suruca deposits, about 6 kilometres apart, and the nearby Saúva project, about 15 kilometres north of the mine. Mining Data Online describes the preferred model as a metamorphosed porphyry copper-gold system associated with skarn mineralisation. At Chapada, copper-gold mineralisation occurs mainly in biotite-plagioclase gneiss and biotite schist, with quartz veinlets containing magnetite and chalcopyrite. Ore minerals are mostly chalcopyrite, pyrite and magnetite, with mineralisation extending over a footprint about 10.5 kilometres long, up to 1.5 kilometres wide and about 380 metres deep. Suruca includes gold-only oxide and sulphide zones as well as copper-gold mineralisation. Additional issues include water management, tailings and broader Brazilian mining controversies. Lundin says Chapada has recently relied on mine drainage, rainfall and recycled process water, and that all electrical supply is from clean energy sources. Mining Data Online reports annual water consumption of about 3.4 million cubic metres, potentially rising to 7.0 million cubic metres with expansions, and roughly 86 percent recycled process water. The supplied controversy links mostly concern other Brazilian mining regions, including illegal Amazon gold mining and lithium exploration near quilombola communities, so they are best treated as wider sector context rather than Chapada-specific allegations.

Cerro Vanguardia Mine is a gold and silver mine in Santa Cruz Province, southern Argentina, extracting gold as the main product and silver as a by-product. It is located about 150 kilometres northwest of Puerto San Julián and is operated by Cerro Vanguardia Sociedad Anónima, owned 92.5 percent by AngloGold Ashanti and 7.5 percent by Fomento Minero de Santa Cruz Sociedad del Estado. Mining began in 1998, and the operation combines open-pit and underground mining across an area of more than 50,000 hectares. Geologically, Cerro Vanguardia lies in the Deseado Massif, a major Jurassic volcanic province in Patagonia. It is a low-sulphidation epithermal precious-metal system. Gold and silver mineralisation occurs in steeply dipping quartz veins that cut relatively flat-lying ignimbrites and volcaniclastic rocks. Mining Data Online reports more than 100 gold- and silver-bearing epithermal veins with a combined exposed strike length of more than 240 kilometres, of which 55 veins are known to contain economic mineralisation. Mineralisation is interpreted as related to sub-volcanic intrusions of the Cerro León Formation. Additional issues include closure planning, tailings management, employment transition and environmental review. Cerro Vanguardia is approaching scheduled closure around 2028, prompting debate over extending mine life through new discoveries while protecting local employment. Recent Argentine reporting says Santa Cruz authorities approved a mine-closure proposal, with the mine expected to finish after about 30 years of gold and silver production. AngloGold Ashanti also discloses a tailings storage facility that began operating in 1998 and has been built in stages. Its 2024 operational profile reported zero reportable environmental incidents, but continued water use, cyanide use, rehabilitation liabilities and tailings closure remain important long-term concerns.

Gualcamayo Mine is a gold mine in northern San Juan Province, Argentina, extracting gold as its principal mineral and producing doré. It is operated by Minas Argentinas and has historically used open-pit and underground mining, heap leaching and processing of ore from the Quebrada del Diablo, Las Vacas and Salamanca areas. The company now describes activity ranging from secondary gold recovery from leach pads to development of the Deep Carbonates Project, which it says could produce about 3,730 kilograms of gold per year for at least 17 years. Geologically, Gualcamayo is a carbonate-hosted gold district with several mineralisation styles. Mining Data Online identifies three main economic types: sediment-hosted distal-disseminated gold at Quebrada del Diablo; sulphide-bearing skarn deposits with copper, zinc and molybdenum plus later gold-arsenic mineralisation at Amelia Inés and Magdalena; and porphyry-style molybdenum mineralisation at Quebrada Perdida. Gold occurs mainly in carbonate breccias and fractured limestone and is linked to hydrothermal fluids moving through faults, dykes, marbles and limestones. Scientific work has compared the deposits to Carlin-like systems because of carbonate host rocks, disseminated gold, arsenian pyrite, alteration and hydrothermal-fluid features. Additional issues include expansion, regulation and safety. In 2025, Reuters reported that Gualcamayo was seeking a 613 million euro investment under Argentina’s Large Investment Incentive Regime, after scaling back an earlier proposal, to advance a new project. Safety concerns are part of the mine’s record: in 2018, two contractor employees died in an accident involving a light truck on an access road at the Las Vacas exploration site.

Salar de Olaroz Mine is a lithium brine operation in Jujuy Province, northwestern Argentina, extracting lithium and producing lithium carbonate for battery and industrial markets. The operation lies in the Olaroz–Cauchari salt-flat basin of the high Andes and has supplied lithium carbonate since 2014. Rio Tinto now describes Olaroz as one of its key brine-based lithium facilities, held through Sales de Jujuy in a joint venture owned 66.5 percent by Rio Tinto, 25 percent by Toyota Tsusho Corporation and 8.5 percent by Jujuy Energía y Minería Sociedad del Estado. Geologically, Olaroz is part of a closed, high-altitude evaporite basin in the Central Andes. Lithium occurs dissolved in brines held within porous salt, clay, sand and gravel aquifers beneath the salar surface. Scientific work describes Salar de Olaroz as a lithium-rich brine system influenced by evaporation, groundwater inflow, surface waters, rivers, tributary streams and water–rock interaction in the surrounding volcanic and sedimentary Andean catchment. The brine is pumped to the surface and concentrated in evaporation ponds before processing into lithium carbonate. Additional issues include water use, Indigenous rights, corporate ownership and transparency. Mining Data Online reports water-use efficiency figures of 42.9 cubic metres per metric tonne of lithium carbonate in 2023 and 49.9 cubic metres per metric tonne in 2022, while noting that Rio Tinto stopped separate Olaroz output reporting after acquiring Arcadium Lithium in March 2025. Global Press Journal reported community allegations that lithium activity in Jujuy has contributed to shrinking water sources and drying wetlands; provincial authorities denied that mining is drying water reserves.

Salar del Hombre Muerto Mine is a lithium brine mining district in the high Andes of northwestern Argentina, extracting mainly lithium from brines and producing lithium carbonate and lithium chloride, with borates also present in the broader salar. The salar spans parts of Catamarca and Salta Provinces and covers about 588 square kilometres. Rio Tinto’s Fénix facility, formerly associated with Arcadium Lithium and Livent, has produced lithium carbonate from high-quality brine at Salar del Hombre Muerto since 1997. Geologically, Salar del Hombre Muerto is an isolated endorheic evaporite basin in the Argentine Puna. It is bounded by crystalline metamorphic basement and subdivided by fault-bounded bedrock highs into eastern and western sub-basins. Mindat describes the eastern basin as borate-rich and the western basin as halite-rich. Lithium occurs dissolved in subsurface brines within evaporite, salt, clay, sand and gravel aquifers, formed by long-term evaporation, groundwater inflow and water–rock interaction in the surrounding volcanic and sedimentary catchments. NASA describes the salar as a high-altitude salt pan rich in minerals such as lithium. Additional issues include water scarcity, Indigenous rights, cumulative environmental impacts and permitting disputes. Environmental justice reporting says the salar is a high-altitude wetland shared by Catamarca and Salta and that multiple lithium projects now cover nearly the whole basin, while impact studies have allegedly failed to assess cumulative and synergistic effects adequately. Community and legal complaints focus on brine pumping, freshwater use, drying rivers and wetlands, and inadequate consultation. A 2024 court ruling reportedly suspended new lithium permits in the area after claims of serious impacts on local water sources and livelihoods.

Chuquicamata Mine is a giant copper mine near Calama in northern Chile’s Antofagasta Region, extracting mainly copper, with associated molybdenum, gold, silver and other industrial metals. It is owned and operated by Corporación Nacional del Cobre de Chile, Chile’s state copper company. The mine is one of the world’s largest open-pit excavations and has been transitioning to underground mining to continue exploiting deeper ore. United States Geological Survey imagery describes the main pit as about 1 kilometre deep, 3 kilometres wide and 5 kilometres long, in the hyper-arid Atacama Desert. Geologically, Chuquicamata is a porphyry copper-molybdenum deposit in the Late Eocene to Early Oligocene porphyry copper belt of northern Chile, a belt extending about 1,400 kilometres along the Andes. The deposit is strongly controlled by the West Fault, part of the Domeyko Fault System, which separates eastern and western structural domains. Mineralisation is related to porphyritic intrusions, hydrothermal alteration, faulting and supergene enrichment, with important copper minerals including chalcopyrite and chalcocite. Additional issues include worker health, town relocation, waste, water and long-term social impacts. As waste dumps expanded and pollution concerns grew, the historic mining town of Chuquicamata was moved; residents began relocating in 2004, and the town was officially abandoned in September 2007 after the company built more than 5,000 replacement homes in Calama. NASA notes that Chuquicamata has transitioned underground to continue extracting metals from one of the world’s deepest open pits. Historical research on the former company town has also examined mortality and extractivism, underlining the mine’s long human and environmental legacy in the Atacama Desert.

Escondida Mine is a giant open-pit copper mine in the Atacama Desert of northern Chile, extracting mainly copper, with associated gold and silver. It is located about 170 kilometres southeast of Antofagasta and is operated by BHP, with Rio Tinto and Japan Escondida Corporation holding minority interests. Rio Tinto describes Escondida as the world’s largest copper mine, producing about 1 million metric tonnes of copper per year, or roughly 6 percent of global production. The mine also uses renewable electricity and has shifted strongly toward desalinated seawater for operations. Geologically, Escondida is a porphyry copper deposit in Chile’s Late Eocene to Early Oligocene copper belt. The main ore body is “hidden” beneath hundreds of metres of barren cover, which explains the Spanish name Escondida. The deposit is part of a north–south cluster of porphyry copper systems associated with the West Fissure fault system. Mineralisation is linked to quartz monzonite to granodiorite intrusions and hydrothermal alteration. A leached cap overlies enriched supergene copper zones dominated by chalcocite and covellite, with deeper primary sulphide mineralisation including chalcopyrite, bornite and pyrite. Additional issues include labour conflict, water stress and environmental regulation. In 2022, workers threatened strike action over safety concerns, following earlier labour tensions at the mine. Water has been especially controversial in the hyper-arid Atacama. Chile’s environmental regulator charged Escondida in 2020 with extracting more water than permitted for nearly 15 years, and later reporting said the case concerned damage to aquifers and wetlands near the Salar de Atacama. BHP says Escondida has ceased operational groundwater extraction and now relies heavily on desalination, but desalination brings its own energy, coastal and brine-disposal concerns.

Collahuasi Mine is a large open-pit copper mine in the high Andes of Tarapacá Region, northern Chile, extracting copper and molybdenum concentrates. It is operated by Compañía Minera Doña Inés de Collahuasi, owned by Anglo American, Glencore and Mitsui. The operation lies about 180 kilometres southeast of Iquique at about 4,400 metres elevation. Collahuasi describes itself as one of the six largest copper producers in the world and the second largest in Chile, with one of the planet’s largest copper deposits, containing about 10,380 million metric tonnes of mineral resources. Geologically, Collahuasi is a cluster of porphyry copper-molybdenum deposits, including Ujina, Rosario and Quebrada Blanca, in the Eocene to Oligocene porphyry copper belt of northern Chile. The district is structurally influenced by major north-trending faults, including the Domeyko and Loa fault systems. Mineralisation is related to porphyritic intrusions, hydrothermal alteration and supergene enrichment, with important sulphide minerals including chalcopyrite, chalcocite, pyrite and pyrrhotite. Historical mining in the district began centuries ago and modern porphyry exploration established its world-scale copper potential. Additional issues include water scarcity, energy demand, Indigenous consultation and mine-expansion disputes. Chilean copper mining faces persistent challenges over water and power supply, and Collahuasi has been affected by declining ore grades and water constraints. In 2023, Indigenous communities challenged environmental approval for a project to expand capacity and extend operations by 20 years. In May 2026, Chile’s Second Environmental Court annulled the approval for a 3.2 billion United States dollar expansion and ordered a new review, citing shortcomings in evaluation of human impacts and Indigenous communities.

Radomiro Tomic Mine is an open-pit copper mine near Calama in Chile’s Antofagasta Region, extracting mainly oxidised copper minerals to produce copper cathodes. It is owned and operated by Corporación Nacional del Cobre de Chile, Chile’s state copper company, and lies about 4 kilometres from Chuquicamata at about 3,000 metres elevation in the Atacama Desert. Mining Technology says the mine was the first operation entirely developed by the Chilean state copper company, with development approved in 1995 and the initial hydrometallurgical operation commissioned with capacity of about 180,000 metric tonnes per year of copper cathodes. Geologically, Radomiro Tomic is a porphyry copper deposit in the Chuquicamata district, part of northern Chile’s Late Eocene to Early Oligocene porphyry copper belt. The district includes the Chuquicamata and Radomiro Tomic megadeposits, Mina Sur, Ministro Hales and the Toki cluster. Mineralisation is associated with porphyry intrusions, potassium-silicate alteration, quartz veins and supergene enrichment. The main oxide copper minerals include atacamite, chrysocolla, copper-bearing clays and copper wad, while deeper sulphide resources include chalcopyrite and molybdenite-bearing zones. Codelco reports sulphide reserves of about 1,390 million metric tonnes at an average copper grade of 0.5 percent. Additional issues include tailings, groundwater, expansion and long-term desert impacts. The Talabre tailings dam receives waste from Codelco’s Chuquicamata, Ministro Hales and Radomiro Tomic divisions, and a 2016 legal action by Loa River water defenders sought to halt its expansion over alleged health and environmental risks near Calama, Chiu-Chiu and Lasana. Chilean environmental authorities later charged Codelco over noncompliance linked to groundwater-protection measures at Talabre. Codelco has also proposed extending Radomiro Tomic leaching operations to 2058, requiring pit expansion, waste dumps and stockpiles.

Candelaria Mine is a copper, gold and silver mining complex near Tierra Amarilla, about 20 kilometres south of Copiapó in Chile’s Atacama Region. It is operated by Lundin Mining, which indirectly owns 80 percent, with Sumitomo holding 20 percent. The complex includes the Candelaria open pit and underground mines, plus the nearby Ojos del Salado underground operations, and produces copper concentrate containing gold and silver. Lundin describes Candelaria as its largest operation. Geologically, Candelaria is an iron oxide copper gold deposit in the Punta del Cobre district. Mindat places it within the contact-metamorphic aureole of a granodioritic batholith exposed about 1 kilometre west of the deposit. Mining Data Online describes copper-gold sulphide mineralisation as iron oxide copper gold, occurring as veins, hydrothermal breccias, replacement mantos and calcic skarns, depending on lithology and structural setting. Main ore minerals include chalcopyrite with magnetite, pyrite, pyrrhotite and sphalerite, and mineralisation is associated with potassic alteration, foliation-parallel veinlets, breccia bodies and mantos. Additional issues include water supply, industrial emissions, blasting and regulatory compliance. The mine operates in the arid Atacama and has used a reverse-osmosis desalination plant at Caldera, piping industrial water about 115 kilometres to the mine. In 2020, Chile’s environmental regulator filed six charges against Lundin’s Candelaria mine, including alleged liquid industrial waste violations, water-consumption issues and use of more explosives than allowed between August 2019 and April 2020.

Spence Mine is an open-pit copper mine in the Sierra Gorda commune of Chile’s Antofagasta Region, extracting copper from oxide and sulphide ore. It is part of BHP’s wholly owned Pampa Norte business, together with Cerro Colorado. Spence began operations in 2006 and lies in the Atacama Desert, about 162 kilometres northeast of Antofagasta. BHP says Pampa Norte produces high-quality copper cathode using leaching, solvent extraction and electrowinning, while the Spence Growth Option added sulphide concentration capacity. Reported 2024 output was about 255,600 metric tonnes of copper. Geologically, Spence is a porphyry copper deposit in northern Chile’s Paleocene copper belt. The deposit is related to dacitic porphyry stocks and tourmaline-bearing hydrothermal breccias emplaced into Upper Cretaceous to Lower Tertiary andesitic volcanic rocks. Hypogene mineralisation formed about 56 million to 57 million years ago and includes chalcopyrite, pyrite, molybdenite, lesser bornite and tennantite. The ore system includes supergene-enriched and hypogene zones, with oxide copper minerals such as atacamite and chrysocolla important near surface. Additional issues include water, automation, permitting and labour relations. Water supply is a central concern in the hyper-arid Atacama; engineering work for Spence has included water and wastewater systems, and BHP has invested heavily in desalination and modernised processing across its Chilean copper assets. BHP has also described Spence as a highly automated operation, with autonomous haul trucks and drills, raising questions about workforce transition as well as safety benefits. In 2025, the Spence workers’ union filed a multiple employer lawsuit against BHP-controlled companies, alleging common labour direction across related operations.

Quebrada Blanca Mine is a high-altitude open-pit copper and molybdenum mine in Tarapacá Region, northern Chile, extracting copper as its main mineral and molybdenum as a by-product. It is operated by Compañía Minera Teck Quebrada Blanca and lies in the western Andes near the Bolivian border, about 165 kilometres southeast of Iquique. The original operation mined near-surface supergene copper ore, while the expanded Quebrada Blanca Phase 2 project is developing deeper sulphide resources. Teck says the new concentrator can process 140,000 metric tonnes per day of ore, using crushing, milling, flotation, thickening and filtering. Geologically, Quebrada Blanca is a typical Andean porphyry copper-molybdenum deposit. It lies in northern Chile’s major porphyry copper belt, close to the Collahuasi district. Mineralisation is linked to porphyry intrusions, hydrothermal alteration and supergene enrichment. The earlier mine exploited oxidised and enriched copper zones, while the current expansion targets primary sulphide mineralisation below the older pit. Mining Data Online notes that sedimentary rocks of the Quehuita Formation unconformably overlie the Collahuasi Formation near the deposit. Additional issues include water use, tailings, Indigenous concerns and regulatory disputes. Phase 2 includes a port, desalination plant, concentrate pipeline and desalinated-water pipeline, reducing reliance on scarce inland freshwater but adding coastal and marine-management concerns. Environmental justice groups have raised concerns about impacts on Indigenous Aymara communities, water resources and high-Andean ecosystems. In 2026, Chile’s environmental regulator approved a compliance programme after charging the company with eight environmental infractions, four serious and four minor. Reuters-linked reporting also said Chilean authorities and workers had flagged cracks and water leaks at the tailings dam in 2025.

Peñasquito Mine is a large open-pit polymetallic mine in Zacatecas, Mexico, extracting gold, silver, lead and zinc. It is owned and operated by Newmont Corporation and lies about 780 kilometres northwest of Mexico City. Newmont says the property began production in 2009 and reached commercial production in 2010. Wheaton Precious Metals describes Peñasquito as Mexico’s largest gold mine, its second-largest silver mine, and one of the country’s largest zinc and lead producers; its plant has two sulphide processing lines and a high-pressure grinding-roll circuit with combined capacity of about 110,000 metric tonnes per day. Geologically, Peñasquito is in the Concepción del Oro district and is centred on the Peñasco and Brecha Azul diatreme breccia pipes. The district is dominated by Mesozoic sedimentary rocks intruded by Tertiary granodiorite and quartz monzonite stocks, then overlain by younger sedimentary rocks and alluvium. Mineralisation includes gold, silver, lead and zinc in breccia-hosted sulphide systems, plus deeper garnet skarn-hosted copper-gold-silver-zinc-lead mineralisation and carbonate-replacement-style zones between the Peñasco and Brecha Azul diatremes. Additional issues include labour conflict, water disputes, local protests and mine-life extension. A four-month strike that began in June 2023 ended after workers accepted an 8 percent pay increase; production losses were substantial. Earlier blockades involved landowners, truckers and community members, with reported demands over jobs, compensation for environmental damage and clean water; Goldcorp said some protests were illegal and linked to contractor disputes. Recent reporting says Peñasquito is also seeking to extend its operating life, making water, tailings, labour relations and community benefits continuing concerns.

Buenavista Mine, also known as Buenavista del Cobre or the Cananea Mine, is a large active open-pit mine immediately north of Cananea in Sonora, Mexico, extracting mainly copper, with molybdenum, silver and zinc also reported. It is operated by Operadora de Minas e Instalaciones Mineras, a subsidiary within Southern Copper Corporation and Grupo México. Mining Data Online describes Buenavista as one of the world’s largest copper ore deposits, with two copper concentrators, a zinc concentrator and two solvent-extraction and electrowinning plants; production began in 1899 and the mine life is listed to 2065. Geologically, Buenavista is Mexico’s largest porphyry copper deposit in the Cananea mining district. Mindat identifies it as a porphyry copper-molybdenum deposit and lists copper as the principal commodity, with minerals including chalcocite, chalcopyrite, covellite, molybdenite, pyrite and quartz. Mining Data Online describes mineralisation centred on quartz monzonite intrusions and breccia chimneys, with high-grade brecciated bodies such as La Colorada hosting copper, gold and silver. The Buenavista Zinc deposit lies northwest of the main copper deposit and is described as a skarn-style polymetallic system containing zinc, copper and silver. Additional issues include labour conflict, tailings risk and the long aftermath of Mexico’s worst mining spill. In 2014, about 40,000 cubic metres of acidified copper sulphate entered the Bacanuchi and Sonora rivers, affecting about 22,000 people in seven municipalities. The organization Earthworks has also criticised expansion of the tailings dam, describing plans for a structure about 10 kilometres long, 175 metres tall and covering 33 square kilometres. Former workers and union members have also raised claims over historic payments and unsafe working conditions.

Los Filos Mine is a gold and silver mine in Guerrero, Mexico, extracting mainly gold, with silver as a by-product. It lies in the municipality of Eduardo Neri, about 180 kilometres south of Mexico City. The operation includes three open pits, Los Filos, Bermejal and Guadalupe, and two underground mines, Los Filos and Bermejal. Ore has been processed by heap leaching to produce gold doré on site, with crushed ore leached for about 120 days and run-of-mine ore for about 180 days. Equinox Gold suspended operations indefinitely on 1 April 2025 after its land-access agreement with Carrizalillo expired. Geologically, Los Filos is an intrusion-related gold-silver iron-oxide skarn system. Mineralisation is associated with early Tertiary granodiorite stocks intruded into carbonate rocks, producing calc-silicate and oxide skarn alteration, followed by mesothermal to epithermal alteration. Mining Data Online reports hematite and magnetite as typical skarn minerals, with gold and silver mineralisation hosted around the Los Filos and Bermejal deposit areas. Regionally, the district contains skarn-hosted, epithermal precious-metal oxide and volcanic-hosted massive-sulphide mineralisation. Additional issues include land tenure, community consent, employment, closure responsibilities and security. MiningWatch Canada says the 2025 suspension followed the lapse of land-use and social-cooperation agreements with Carrizalillo, whose residents have demanded compensation and proper closure. Mongabay reported that community members maintained a 24-hour encampment outside the mine gates and said some homes are less than 400 metres from a leach pad and between 900 metres and 1.5 kilometres from blasting areas. Equinox says long-term agreements with all three local communities are necessary for any restart and future investment.

Mulatos Gold Mine is an open-pit and developing underground gold operation in the Mulatos district of Sahuaripa, Sonora, Mexico, extracting gold as its main mineral and producing doré, a gold-silver alloy. It is operated by Minas de Oro Nacional, a Mexican subsidiary of Alamos Gold. The district is in the Sierra Madre Occidental, about 220 kilometres east of Hermosillo, and includes La Yaqui Grande and the Puerto Del Aire development project. Commercial production began in 2006 after the first gold pour in 2005. Geologically, Mulatos is a volcanic-hosted, high-sulphidation epithermal gold system. Mineralisation occurs in altered dacite and rhyodacite volcanic rocks of the northern Sierra Madre Occidental. Gold is associated with pyrite and enargite in vuggy silica, pyrophyllite, diaspore and dickite alteration zones, with hypogene mineralisation linked to rhyodacite domes and major faults. The district includes multiple deposits and satellite systems, including Mulatos, Cerro Pelón, La Yaqui, El Carricito, El Halcón, Las Carboneras, El Jaspe, Puebla, Los Bajíos and La Dura. Additional issues include safety, environmental justice concerns, community conflict and security. A 2018 landslide killed workers after earlier warnings about instability, and environmental justice reporting has linked Alamos operations in Sonora to disputes over mine impacts, cyanide risks and community grievances. In April 2020, five heavily armed people robbed gold-silver bars from the Mulatos airstrip and fled in a small aircraft, highlighting insecurity around precious-metals transport in Sonora.

La Herradura Mine is a large open-pit gold mine in Caborca Municipality, Sonora, Mexico, extracting gold, with silver as a secondary metal in doré production. It is operated by Minera Penmont, a Fresnillo subsidiary, and Fresnillo describes Herradura as one of Mexico’s largest open-pit gold mines, contributing 59.3 percent of the group’s gold output in 2025. Mindat lists the locality as a gold mine in the Altar Desert region of northwestern Sonora. Geologically, La Herradura is part of the Sonoran gold belt, a northwest-trending belt of orogenic gold deposits in northwestern Mexico. Mineralisation is hosted mainly in deformed metamorphic and intrusive rocks, with gold occurring in structurally controlled quartz-vein and shear-zone systems. Mining Data Online classifies the deposit as a mesothermal or orogenic gold system, with open-pit mining of near-surface oxide ore and processing by heap leaching and dynamic leaching. The mine area includes the Herradura, Centauro and related deposits, reflecting a district-scale mineralised system rather than a single isolated ore body. Additional issues include cyanide management, labour conflict and land-rights disputes. The International Cyanide Management Institute reported that La Herradura achieved its fifth certification under the International Cyanide Management Code, indicating audited compliance with cyanide-management standards. However, social controversy has been significant. In 2023, workers protested over an alleged refusal to pay 10 percent of profits said to be owed to about 1,800 workers; Business and Human Rights Resource Centre reported that police removed blockades at the mine entrances after 14 days. Land conflicts with El Bajío community members have also been reported, including allegations of dispossession, contamination, legal harassment and violence, which the company and its supporters have disputed in different forums.

Sukari Mine is a large gold mine in Egypt’s Eastern Desert, near the Red Sea and about 25 kilometres from Marsa Alam, extracting gold as its principal mineral. It is Egypt’s first modern large-scale gold mine and combines a bulk-tonnage open pit with higher-grade underground mining. AngloGold Ashanti describes Sukari as a modern operation in the Arabian-Nubian Shield; it became the owner after acquiring Centamin, while Egypt retains a major economic interest through the profit-sharing structure. Recent reporting places output around 14,000 to 15,500 kilograms of gold per year. Geologically, Sukari is a world-class orogenic gold deposit hosted mainly by the deformed Sukari tonalite-trondhjemite intrusion within a Neoproterozoic accretionary complex. Gold occurs chiefly in networks of crosscutting sulphide-bearing quartz and quartz-carbonate veins, as well as in intensely sulphidised, silicified and sericitised wall rock. The deposit is structurally controlled by thrusting, shearing and folding, with mineralisation extending to more than 1,000 metres depth in some studies. Additional issues include labour unrest, ownership controversy and questions about national benefit. In 2012, workers continued protests amid allegations of corruption, smuggling and labour disputes; separate reporting said about 1,500 workers staged a sit-in over layoffs and pay demands. The 2024 AngloGold Ashanti acquisition of Centamin also drew criticism in Egypt, with some commentators alleging possible “foul play” because Sukari is the country’s flagship gold asset and the deal shifted control to a foreign multinational. Environmental management, water supply from the Red Sea pipeline, waste disposal, worker safety and revenue transparency remain continuing public-interest concerns.

Guelb El Rhein Mine is an open-pit iron ore mine near Zouérate in Tiris Zemmour Region, northern Mauritania, extracting mainly magnetite-rich iron ore. It is operated by Société Nationale Industrielle et Minière, Mauritania’s majority state-owned mining company, and forms part of the country’s northern iron ore system with the Kédia d’Idjil and M’Haoudat mines. The Guelb II expansion was described by the African Development Bank as the largest industrial project in Mauritania’s history, adding beneficiation capacity, power infrastructure and related mine expansion. Geologically, Guelb El Rhein lies in the F’derik–Zouérate iron district of the Reguibat Shield, part of the West African Craton. The iron formations are Archean to Paleoproterozoic rocks of the Tiris Complex, including banded iron formation and magnetite quartzite. Unlike higher-grade hematite ores elsewhere in the district, Guelb El Rhein contains lower-grade magnetite ore that requires crushing, grinding and beneficiation before export. Industry reporting describes raw ore around 35.4 percent iron, with mass recovery around 42 percent, while related technical summaries describe magnetite concentrate production of about 4 million metric tonnes per year before the Guelb II expansion. Additional issues include heavy dependence on rail, port and energy infrastructure; exposure to global iron ore prices; and performance risks from large-scale beneficiation. The United States Geological Survey reported that Mauritania’s iron ore output and exports fell in 2018 partly because of lower-than-expected production from the Guelb II plant and rehabilitation work at Guelb I. Broader concerns include the environmental footprint of open-pit mining, waste rock, water demand in an arid region, dust, energy use and the economic concentration of Mauritania’s mining sector around Société Nationale Industrielle et Minière infrastructure.

Tasiast Gold Mine is a large open-pit gold mine in northwestern Mauritania, extracting gold as its principal mineral. It is operated by Tasiast Mauritanie Limited Société Anonyme, a wholly owned subsidiary of Kinross Gold Corporation, which acquired the operation in 2010. The mine lies in the Inchiri and Dakhlet Nouadhibou regions, about 300 kilometres north of Nouakchott and about 250 kilometres southeast of Nouadhibou. Mining began in 2007, and the operation uses conventional open-pit mining with crushing, grinding, carbon-in-leach processing and dump leaching. Kinross expanded the plant to reach about 24,000 metric tonnes per day of processing capacity. Geologically, Tasiast is an orogenic gold system in the Aouéouat greenstone belt of the Reguibat Shield, part of the West African Craton. The principal deposits include West Branch, Piment, Prolongation and Fennec. Gold mineralisation is associated with the west-vergent Tasiast shear system, where mafic to felsic volcanic and intrusive rocks of the Aouéouat assemblage are thrust over younger metasedimentary rocks of the Tasiast assemblage. The gold trend runs broadly north–south through the mining permit, with mineralisation in shear zones, altered volcanic and intrusive rocks, and structurally controlled quartz-vein systems. Additional issues include labour relations, fiscal scrutiny, security and corruption concerns. Civil-society reporting has raised suspicions about corruption and internal-control weaknesses linked to Kinross’s Mauritania and Ghana operations, citing United States regulatory inquiries. Labour advocates have also criticised working conditions and argued that Mauritania’s gold sector needs fairer employment practices. In the desert setting, continuing concerns include water supply, cyanide management, tailings, waste rock, dust, long-distance logistics and equitable national benefit from a foreign-operated mine.

Benguerir Phosphates Mine is an open-pit phosphate mine about 70 kilometres north of Marrakesh, Morocco, extracting phosphate rock used mainly to produce phosphoric acid and fertilisers. It is operated by the Office Chérifien des Phosphates Group and is managed with the nearby Youssoufia mining and treatment centre in the Ganntour phosphate basin. Production began in 1979–1980, making Benguerir one of Morocco’s newer major phosphate centres. Morocco is central to the global fertiliser supply chain and holds the world’s largest phosphate rock reserves, estimated by the United States Geological Survey at about 50 billion metric tonnes, far above any other country. Geologically, Benguerir belongs to the Ganntour basin, an east–west sedimentary basin about 120 kilometres long and 20 to 30 kilometres wide. The exploited phosphate series ranges from the Maastrichtian to the Lutetian and rests on older basement rocks. It consists of layered clays, marls, limestones, cherts and phosphate-rich beds. Mineable layers are commonly about 1 to 3 metres thick and grade roughly 22 to 28 percent phosphorus pentoxide. The basin has a generally simple monoclinal structure, with slight northwest dip and limited major faulting or folding in studied panels.  Additional issues include waste-rock management, dust, land disturbance, water demand and the wider environmental footprint of fertiliser production. Open-pit phosphate mining at Benguerir generates large volumes of overburden and interburden waste rock; one case study cited about 4.2 million metric tonnes of phosphate rock extracted in 2020 and 12.3 million metric tonnes of waste rock. Morocco’s phosphate industry is also politically sensitive because Office Chérifien des Phosphates operations extend to Western Sahara through Phosboucraa, raising wider concerns over resource governance, sovereignty and benefit sharing.

Khouribga Phosphate Mine is a major open-pit phosphate mining district around Khouribga, Morocco, extracting phosphate rock used to make phosphoric acid and fertilisers. The area is operated by the state-owned Office Chérifien des Phosphates Group. Khouribga’s first mine opened in 1921, and the district remains central to Morocco’s phosphate industry, producing about 70 percent of the group’s output. Morocco also holds the world’s largest phosphate rock reserves, and Khouribga is widely described as one of the world’s largest phosphate mining centres. Phosphate is moved by rail and through a 235-kilometre slurry pipeline to processing and export facilities at Jorf Lasfar and Casablanca. Geologically, Khouribga lies in the Ouled Abdoun Basin, also called the Khouribga Basin, on the Plateau des Phosphates west of the Middle Atlas. The basin is a large sedimentary phosphate province with Late Cretaceous to Eocene phosphorite beds interlayered with clay, limestone, marl and sandstone. The phosphate formed in shallow marine conditions and is mined from layered phosphatic horizons. The region is also famous for abundant marine vertebrate fossils, reflecting deposition under ancient Atlantic seas. Additional issues include water demand, dust, waste rock, worker health and downstream pollution from fertiliser production. To reduce reliance on scarce inland water, Office Chérifien des Phosphates has launched a desalinated-water pipeline of more than 200 kilometres from the Atlantic coast to Khouribga. Controversies include allegations that phosphate mining and processing expose workers and nearby communities to toxic dust, heavy metals and industrial pollution; investigative reporting has highlighted fears of illness among Moroccan phosphate workers, while the company has emphasised sustainability investments and water projects.

Bou Craa Phosphate Mine is an open-pit phosphate mine in Western Sahara, extracting high-purity phosphate rock used mainly for fertiliser production. The mine is operated by Phosboucraa, a subsidiary of Morocco’s Office Chérifien des Phosphates Group, and lies about 100 kilometres inland from El Aaiún. Phosphate deposits were discovered by Spain in 1947, mining began in the 1960s and 1970s, and output in 2001 was about 1.5 million metric tonnes of phosphate. The mine is famous for its 98 to 102 kilometre conveyor belt, often described as the world’s longest, which carries phosphate rock to the coast near El Marsa. Geologically, Bou Craa is a near-surface sedimentary phosphate deposit in the arid Sahara. The phosphate occurs in laterally extensive marine phosphorite beds deposited in ancient shallow-sea environments. NASA notes that the deposits are abundant, pure and close to the surface, making them suitable for large-scale open-pit extraction. The dry desert setting reduces surface-water complications but increases dust and transport concerns. Additional issues are dominated by sovereignty, resource governance and environmental concerns. Western Sahara is a Non-Self-Governing Territory, and Morocco’s control and export of Bou Craa phosphate are disputed by Sahrawi groups and Western Sahara Resource Watch, which argues that the trade occurs without Sahrawi consent. Western Sahara Resource Watch reported that Bou Craa exports have recently represented about 10 percent of Morocco’s phosphate rock exports and estimated the 2024 trade value at about 319 million United States dollars. Environmental concerns include dust from mining and conveyor transport, landscape disturbance, fossil-resource depletion and the broader impacts of phosphate fertiliser supply chains.

Antamina Mine is a large open-pit copper and zinc mine in the Ancash Region of Peru, extracting mainly copper and zinc, with molybdenum, silver, lead and bismuth as by-products. It is operated by Compañía Minera Antamina and owned by BHP, Glencore, Teck Resources and Mitsubishi Corporation. The mine sits at about 4,500 metres elevation in the Andes and is one of the world’s largest copper-zinc operations. Copper and zinc concentrates are transported by a roughly 302-kilometre slurry pipeline to the coast for export. Geologically, Antamina is a giant polymetallic skarn deposit in the western Cordillera of central Peru. The deposit formed where multiple intrusive events altered carbonate rocks, producing copper-, zinc-, molybdenum- and silver-bearing skarn. Mining Data Online describes copper mineralisation mainly as chalcopyrite, with some bornite, while zinc is concentrated especially in exoskarn and molybdenum in endoskarn. Silver overlaps with copper and zinc zones. Scientific descriptions identify Antamina as one of the largest known copper-zinc skarn deposits, with mineralisation controlled by intrusive contacts, alteration zones and structurally prepared carbonate host rocks. Additional issues include water scarcity, pollution allegations, expansion and community conflict. In 2025, The Guardian reported that nearby communities alleged drying water sources, arsenic contamination and deepening poverty linked to the mine; Mitsubishi said Antamina meets local and international standards and prioritises coexistence with communities and the environment. New Internationalist reported that Peru approved a 1.85 billion euro expansion in 2024, expected to raise production by 20 percent in 2026, while campaigners warned of risks to downstream communities from mine water and heavy metals.

Cerro Verde Mine is a large open-pit copper and molybdenum mining complex about 30 kilometres southwest of Arequipa, Peru, extracting mainly copper, with molybdenum and silver as important by-products. It is operated by Sociedad Minera Cerro Verde, with ownership led by Freeport-McMoRan, Sumitomo interests and Compañía de Minas Buenaventura. Freeport-McMoRan describes Cerro Verde as an open-pit copper and molybdenum complex whose major expansion was completed in 2015. Geologically, Cerro Verde is a porphyry copper-molybdenum district in southern Peru, including the Cerro Verde, Santa Rosa and Cerro Negro deposits. Mining Data Online describes it as a copper-molybdenum porphyry cluster related to calc-alkaline dacite to monzonite porphyry intrusions and quartz-tourmaline hydrothermal breccias. Oxide copper minerals include brochantite, chrysocolla, malachite and copper pitch; the main secondary copper sulphides are chalcocite and covellite, while chalcopyrite and molybdenite dominate the primary sulphide zones. Additional issues include water use, air quality, land impacts and community protest. Cerro Verde operates in an arid region near a large urban centre, making water supply and dust control especially sensitive. Business and Human Rights Resource Centre reported that residents of Mollebaya protested outside company offices, accusing the mine of excessive pollution. Mongabay reported that expanded production at the United States-linked mine has been accompanied by dozens of fines, mostly related to dust that residents say has affected nearby communities; the same reporting described broader contamination concerns involving foreign-owned mines in Peru. The mine remains economically important, but its scale, proximity to Arequipa, waste management, groundwater protection and community-health complaints make it a persistent focus of environmental and social scrutiny.

Toquepala Mine is a large open-pit copper mine in Jorge Basadre Province, Tacna Region, southern Peru, extracting mainly copper, with molybdenum, rhenium, silver and minor gold and zinc. It is owned and operated by Southern Peru Copper Corporation, a subsidiary of Grupo México, and has operated since 1960. The operation includes a steep-sided open pit, concentrator facilities and solvent extraction–electrowinning capacity for copper cathode production. Copper from mines such as Toquepala is significant for the energy transition because renewable power systems, electricity grids, electric vehicles, batteries, motors and charging infrastructure all require large amounts of conductive metal. NASA describes Toquepala as one of the major mines of the mineral-rich Andes, visible as a stepped “bullseye” excavation in the arid mountain landscape. Geologically, Toquepala is a classic porphyry copper-molybdenum deposit in the Paleocene to early Eocene metallogenic belt of the southern Peruvian Andes. Mineralisation is associated with porphyritic intrusions, disseminated fine-grained sulphides, abundant anhydrite veins and tourmaline breccias. Mindat describes the mine as a large copper-rhenium-molybdenum porphyry system, while Mining Data Online identifies it as a copper-molybdenum porphyry deposit. Important minerals include chalcopyrite, molybdenite, pyrite and secondary copper minerals, although the oxide zone has reportedly been largely mined out. Additional issues include water stress, tailings, labour and wider conflict around Southern Copper’s Peruvian operations. The mine lies in a dry Andean region where water supply, waste rock, dust and long-term tailings management are persistent concerns. Community protests in Peru have also disrupted Southern Copper operations; in 2022, protests at the nearby Cuajone mine forced the company to consider importing copper concentrate for its Ilo refinery, illustrating broader tensions over water, land, employment and benefit sharing in southern Peru. Recent controversy around the company’s delayed Tía María copper project further shows the difficulty of obtaining and maintaining social acceptance for large copper projects in Peru.

Yanacocha Mine is a large open-pit gold mine complex in Cajamarca Region, northern Peru, extracting gold as its main mineral, with silver and mercury recovered as by-products of processing. It is owned and operated by Newmont and lies about 800 kilometres northeast of Lima, at elevations of about 3,500 to 4,100 metres. Newmont identifies Yanacocha as South America’s largest gold mine; it poured its first gold bar in 1993 and operates across four primary drainage basins. United States Geological Survey satellite analysis describes the mine as a landscape-scale operation with multiple open pits and disturbed areas covering more than 30,000 hectares. Geologically, Yanacocha is one of the world’s largest high-sulphidation epithermal gold districts. It is hosted by the Miocene Yanacocha Volcanic Complex in the northern Andes, where andesitic to dacitic volcanic rocks, domes, porphyry intrusions and pyroclastic units were intensely altered by hydrothermal fluids. Gold mineralisation is associated with advanced argillic alteration, silicified rocks, breccias, faults and disseminated sulphides. The district contains multiple deposits rather than a single ore body, including Yanacocha, Maqui Maqui, Carachugo, Cerro Negro and La Quinua. Additional issues include water conflict, land disturbance, cyanide leaching and long-term community distrust. Earthworks has criticised Yanacocha for alleged impacts on water, farming communities and high-Andean ecosystems. The most notorious controversy is the 2 June 2000 Choropampa mercury spill, when a truck carrying mercury from Yanacocha released more than 150 kilograms along roads through Choropampa and nearby villages; residents have reported continuing health and social impacts decades later. Newmont emphasises environmental management and continued operations, but Yanacocha remains a symbol of both the economic importance and social risks of large-scale gold mining in Peru.

Ingulets Mine is a large open-pit iron ore mine in Kryvyi Rih, Dnipropetrovsk Oblast, central Ukraine, extracting iron ore from ferruginous quartzite and producing high-grade iron ore concentrate for steelmaking. It is part of Metinvest’s Kryvyi Rih mining and processing business. The Ingulets Mining and Processing Plant is described as Ukraine’s largest producer of 64 to 67 percent iron concentrate, with iron ore reserves of about 1.2 billion metric tonnes; other summaries list about 937 million metric tonnes grading 63.5 percent iron. Geologically, Ingulets belongs to the Kryvyi Rih iron ore basin, one of eastern Europe’s major Precambrian banded iron formation districts. The mine develops the Ingulets deposit of low-grade magnetite ores, especially magnetite quartzites. Geological studies describe a complex productive sequence in the Saksagan suite of the Kryvyi Rih series, including multiple ferruginous and schistose horizons. The mined ore is therefore not simply massive iron ore, but a layered metamorphosed iron formation requiring crushing, grinding, magnetic separation and beneficiation to produce iron concentrate. Additional issues include war-related disruption, export pressure, energy costs, infrastructure risk and environmental impacts from large-scale open-pit mining, waste rock, tailings and dust. Global Energy Monitor currently lists the Metinvest Ingulets Mine as mothballed, while industry reporting says Metinvest has a plan to restart Ingulets Mining but is waiting for more stable domestic and export markets. Ukraine’s iron ore sector remains under pressure: exports fell 33.9 percent year on year in the first quarter of 2026 to 5.61 million metric tonnes, amid logistics constraints and weaker demand. Despite this, Metinvest’s Central, Ingulets and Northern mining and processing plants paid 1.3 billion hryvnias in taxes in the first quarter of 2026, highlighting the sector’s fiscal importance during wartime.

Central Iron Ore Enrichment Works, also known as Central Mining and Processing Plant or Central GOK, is an iron ore mining and processing complex in Kryvyi Rih, Dnipropetrovsk Oblast, Ukraine. It extracts magnetite-rich iron ore and produces iron ore concentrate and pellets for steelmaking. The operation belongs to Metinvest and is unusual in Ukraine because it uses both open-pit and underground mining, drawing ore from the Hleyevatskyi, Petrovskyi and Artyomovskyi open pits and the Ordzhonikidze underground mine. Its products include concentrate grading 67.5 to 68.2 percent iron and pellets grading about 65 percent iron. Total magnetite quartzite reserves are estimated at about 463 million metric tonnes. Geologically, the operation is part of the Kryvyi Rih iron ore basin, a major Precambrian banded iron formation district in central Ukraine. The ore consists mainly of magnetite quartzite within metamorphosed iron-rich strata of the Kryvyi Rih series. These layered rocks require crushing, grinding, magnetic separation, concentration and pelletising before use in blast furnaces or other ironmaking routes. The combination of open pits and underground workings reflects the depth, structure and distribution of the ferruginous quartzite bodies. Additional issues include wartime disruption, export logistics, power supply, regulation and environmental impacts from pits, tailings, dust and waste rock. Despite Russia’s war against Ukraine, Central GOK reportedly increased output in 2024 and reached almost full capacity by year-end. Metinvest also reorganised its Kryvyi Rih mining enterprises under a unified management structure in 2024. Controversies include complaints from industry groups that law-enforcement pressure and subsoil regulation have weakened the mining investment climate, alongside wider concerns over Ukraine’s energy-sector corruption crisis and wartime industrial resilience.

Ferrexpo Poltava Mine is a large open-pit iron ore mine at Horishni Plavni in Poltava Oblast, central Ukraine, extracting magnetite iron ore and producing iron ore concentrate and pellets for steelmaking. It is operated by Ferrexpo Poltava Mining, part of the London-listed Ferrexpo group. The mine supplies ore to an adjacent processing and pelletising plant; Ferrexpo says Poltava mined about 80 million metric tonnes of material in 2021, including 17 million metric tonnes of high-grade iron ore for processing. The company describes its Ukrainian operations as producing high-quality pellets for global steelmakers. Geologically, Poltava is part of the Kremenchuk magnetic anomaly and the Precambrian iron formations of the Ukrainian Shield. The mine exploits the Gorishne-Plavninskoe and Lavrikovskoe deposits, which contain magnetite-rich banded iron formation, commonly described as ferruginous quartzite. The ore is low enough in natural grade to require crushing, grinding, magnetic separation and pelletising, but beneficiation produces high-grade iron ore pellets suitable for steelmaking. NS Energy reports mining to about 300 metres depth and notes that mining began in 1960, with concentrate production starting in 1970 and pellet production in 1977. Additional issues include Russia’s war against Ukraine, power and logistics disruption, legal disputes and state intervention. Ferrexpo restarted one pelletising line in March 2026 after a temporary suspension caused by energy-supply constraints. In February 2025, Reuters reported that Ukraine had moved to nationalise Poltava Mining amid an investigation into alleged misappropriation of funds linked to illegal mining, while Ferrexpo’s shares fell sharply. In 2026, a Ukrainian court also opened bankruptcy proceedings against Ferrexpo Poltava Mining over a disputed 4.727 billion hryvnia claim tied to surety agreements; Ferrexpo said it would appeal.

Muruntau Gold Mine is a vast open-pit gold mine in the Qizilqum Desert of central Uzbekistan, extracting gold as its main mineral. It is owned and operated by Navoi Mining and Metallurgical Company, Uzbekistan’s state-owned gold producer. The deposit was discovered in 1958 and mining began in 1967. NASA describes Muruntau as one of the largest single gold deposits on Earth, with an open pit about 3.5 kilometres wide and 600 metres deep; 2021 production was reported at as much as 85,000 kilograms of gold.  Geologically, Muruntau is a giant orogenic-style gold deposit in the Kyzylkum Desert. United States Geological Survey research places the ore in Cambrian to Ordovician rocks of the Besopan Suite, a roughly 5,000-metre-thick turbiditic sequence of siltstone, shale and sandstone. Ore is concentrated mainly near the base of the Besopan-3 unit, a 2,000-metre-thick package of carbonaceous shale, siltstone, sandstone and chert. Gold deposition was controlled by thrust faults, folding, metamorphic permeability and the Sangruntau-Tamdytau shear zone. Additional issues include state ownership, transparency, expansion, labour conditions and desert environmental management. Navoi Mining and Metallurgical Company says it is among the world’s top four gold producers and lists Muruntau as its largest mine, with more than 50,000 employees across the company and a mine life exceeding 30 years. Governance concerns have included reporting that a PricewaterhouseCoopers study found major supply-chain overcharging at Navoi Mining and Metallurgical Company, raising questions about procurement and oversight. In this arid setting, continuing concerns include water demand, heap leaching, cyanide management, waste rock, dust and the social effects of a strategically important state gold industry.

Angren Coal Mine is a large open-pit brown coal, or lignite, mine at Angren in Tashkent Region, eastern Uzbekistan, extracting coal mainly for domestic power generation and industrial use. It is operated by Uzbekcoal Joint Stock Company and is closely linked to the nearby Angren power station, which Global Energy Monitor lists as an operating coal-fired power plant of at least 286 megawatts. Technical literature reports Angren production of about 4.45 million metric tonnes of brown coal per year and notes that the mine began and continues as an open-pit operation. Geologically, Angren lies in the Angren coal basin of eastern Uzbekistan. The mine works brown coal seams within a sedimentary basin that includes Paleozoic basement overlain by Mesozoic and Cenozoic deposits. Studies of the open pit describe Quaternary, Neogene, Cretaceous–Paleogene and Jurassic water-bearing strata and complexes, which strongly affect slope stability. The deposit’s thick, near-surface coal-bearing sequence made large-scale surface mining feasible, but its layered sedimentary rocks, groundwater and tectonic structures create persistent geotechnical challenges. Additional issues include landslide hazards, air pollution, ash disposal, groundwater risk and Uzbekistan’s continuing reliance on coal-fired electricity. Researchers have specifically studied landslide movements and seismic monitoring at the Angren open pit, indicating that slope deformation is a major safety and operational concern. Water-quality research found that a coal fly-ash pond related to Angren coal-fired power plants had high concentrations of arsenic, aluminium, boron, molybdenum and antimony, with potential groundwater-contamination risk through infiltration. The mine and power station remain economically important, but they also illustrate the environmental and climate tensions of coal dependence, including dust, mine-water management, land disturbance, carbon dioxide emissions and long-term reclamation needs.

Chamalang Coal Mines are a group of coal mines in Balochistan, Pakistan, extracting mainly bituminous coal for brick kilns, cement plants and other domestic energy uses. The coalfield extends across parts of Loralai, Kohlu and Barkhan districts and is often described as covering about 1,295 square kilometres. Mining began on a larger scale after a 2006 agreement among local tribal groups, contractors and the provincial government helped reopen access to an area long affected by the Luni–Marri tribal dispute. Reported resources are commonly cited at about 500 million metric tonnes of coal. Geologically, Chamalang belongs to the Sulaiman, or Middle Indus, Basin and is an eastern continuation of the Duki coalfield. The coal occurs in the Paleocene Ghazij Formation, within sedimentary rocks ranging from Jurassic to recent age in the surrounding area. Studies identify coal-bearing areas including Bala Dhaka, Lunda, Surghari, Nosham and Bahlol. The coal ranges from high-volatile C bituminous to high-volatile A bituminous, with deposits formed in ancient coastal to deltaic sedimentary environments. Additional issues include dangerous working conditions, weak regulation, tribal politics, child labour concerns and insecurity in Balochistan.
