Mine safety is a major controversy: reported accidents include landslides killing two miners in Chamalang in 2022 and poisonous or methane gas incidents killing miners in Chamalang and nearby Duki coalfields in 2025. Wider Pakistani coal mining is frequently criticised for poor ventilation, inadequate safety equipment, informal labour arrangements and low wages. While supporters present Chamalang as a development project that created thousands of jobs and reduced tribal conflict, its social benefits are offset by fatal accidents, labour vulnerability, environmental disturbance and dependence on coal in a carbon-constrained energy system.

Saindak Copper-Gold Mine is a copper, gold and silver mine near Saindak in Chagai District, Balochistan, Pakistan. It is owned through Saindak Metals Limited, a Government of Pakistan company, and operated by MCC Resources Development Company, linked to Metallurgical Corporation of China. The project was established in 1995 after copper deposits were identified in the 1970s, and it is often described as Pakistan’s first large-scale non-ferrous metals mine, with mining, concentration and smelting on site. Recent Chinese and Pakistani reporting presents the project as an important source of employment, infrastructure and state revenue in a remote mineral-rich region. Geologically, Saindak is a porphyry copper-gold deposit in the Chagai belt of western Pakistan. The deposit consists of three main ore bodies: south, east and north. Each is centred on ore-forming tonalite and cut by diorite porphyry and andesite dikes. The area is part of the Chagai-Raskoh copper, iron and gold metallogenic zone, with mineralisation related to subduction-linked magmatism, hydrothermal alteration and porphyry-style stockwork and disseminated sulphides. The United States Geological Survey notes that alteration and mineralisation at Saindak have many features typical of porphyry copper deposits. Additional issues include ownership, revenue sharing, Chinese investment and local resentment in Balochistan. Critics argue that the province has not received benefits proportionate to the value of its minerals, while supporters frame Saindak as a development project. German broadcaster Deutsche Welle has reported that Chinese investment has stoked anger in Balochistan over exclusion, security and resource control. Governance concerns also persist: Pakistan’s Auditor General reportedly found that an unauthorised change in sampling methods caused a 27.4 billion Pakistani rupee loss, a claim involving the project operator.

Duddar Mine is an underground lead and zinc mine in Lasbela District, Balochistan, Pakistan, extracting mainly zinc and lead concentrates. It is widely described as Pakistan’s largest underground base-metals mine and is operated by MCC Huaye Duddar Mining Company, a Chinese joint venture. The project reached commercial production in 2015 and has reported annual ore capacity of about 500,000 metric tonnes. Mindat lists reserves of about 50 million metric tonnes of ore grading 3.2 percent lead and 8.6 percent zinc, making Duddar one of Pakistan’s most important non-ferrous metal deposits. Geologically, Duddar is a sediment-hosted lead-zinc deposit in the Lasbela–Khuzdar belt of Balochistan. The deposit is hosted mainly by Jurassic carbonate-shale sequences, including limestones, mudstones and siliciclastic rocks, and is commonly compared with sedimentary exhalative lead-zinc systems. Mineralisation is controlled by stratigraphy, facies changes, debris-flow units and later faulting, with principal ore minerals including sphalerite for zinc and galena for lead. A recent geological source describes Duddar as an unusually young sedimentary exhalative-style lead-zinc deposit hosted by Early Jurassic dark siliciclastic rocks. Additional issues include foreign ownership, local benefit sharing, security and environmental management. Ore and concentrates are transported roughly 200 kilometres toward Karachi for shipment and further processing, linking the mine to national export infrastructure. Duddar is often presented as a success for Chinese investment and Pakistani mineral development, but Balochistan’s resource sector remains controversial because of local grievances over exclusion, security controls and whether mining revenues improve conditions for nearby communities. Risk analysts note that Balochistan’s ports and mineral assets face heightened security risks as United States and Chinese interests grow. Environmental and labour concerns include underground safety, ventilation, mine water, tailings, dust, road haulage and long-term closure planning.
